AI ENGINEERING
FROM
SCRATCH

VOLUME 1: FOUNDATIONS

Claude Code (Opus 4.7)
Codex (GPT 5.5)
Open Code (Kimi k2.6)

  1. What Is AI Engineering?
  2. Setting Up the Toolchain
  3. Linear Algebra You'll Actually Use
  4. Calculus, Probability, and the Math of Learning
  5. Statistics, Sampling, and Spectral Methods

Chapter 1 What Is AI Engineering?

A startup ships a customer-support chatbot on a Tuesday. By Friday the bill from their LLM provider is six times what they projected, the bot has confidently invented a refund policy that doesn’t exist, and a journalist has screenshotted it telling a customer to drink bleach. On Monday the founder asks the engineer who shipped it: what happened?

The honest answer is that the engineer knew how to call the API. They didn’t know how to evaluate the model, didn’t know what prompt caching would have cost-reduced their bill by 70%, didn’t know that retrieval-augmented generation would have grounded the refund policy, didn’t know that a moderation classifier would have caught the bleach incident, and didn’t know that none of these are “advanced” techniques they are table stakes for anyone shipping an LLM in 2026.

This is the gap this book is for. There is now a job called AI engineer, and it is not the same job as machine-learning researcher, data scientist, or backend engineer. It overlaps all three. It has its own primitives, its own failure modes, its own economics, and its own production stack. Most of what you need to know to do it well is not yet in any one book. It is scattered across vendor docs, research papers, conference talks, blog posts from people who’ve been on call when an agent went off the rails, and Twitter threads that disappear when the platform changes its name.

We’re going to put it in one place.

What this book actually is

Three volumes. Roughly 37 chapters. One voice.

  • Volume 1: Foundations. The math you’ll actually use, classical machine learning, deep learning, computer vision, NLP, audio. We build a working mental model of every layer beneath the transformer.
  • Volume 2: Modern AI & LLMs. Transformers from scratch, generative models, reinforcement learning, building and aligning your own LLM, the LLM application stack (RAG, tools, evals, fine-tuning), multimodal models, agents, autonomous systems, multi-agent orchestration.
  • Volume 3: Production. Serving LLMs at scale, the economics of inference, alignment in the wild, red-teaming, governance, and seventeen capstone projects you can build to prove you know what you’re doing.

The reference point is Designing Data-Intensive Applications. The book takes positions. It tells you what to reach for first, what to throw away in production, what’s overhyped, and what’s quietly become the boring default.

What it is not: a survey, a paper digest, a vendor manual, or a hype piece. The reader is here because they already buy that AI is a real thing. What they want is to be made competent in it.

The job nobody wrote a job description for

A useful way to see AI engineering is by what it isn’t.

A machine-learning researcher asks: what’s the next architecture? What objective should we train against? What does this loss landscape look like? Their output is a paper or a checkpoint. Their unit of progress is a benchmark number.

A data scientist asks: what is true in this dataset, and how confident am I? Their output is an analysis or a model trained for a specific business question. Their unit of progress is a stakeholder decision.

A backend engineer asks: how do I serve this request reliably under load, with the right latency, the right consistency, the right cost? Their output is a service. Their unit of progress is a green dashboard.

An AI engineer asks all three of these, every day, about a model they did not train and may not fully understand. They take a frontier-lab artifact (Claude, GPT, an open weight) or they fine-tune one or they build one from open components. They wrap it in retrieval, tools, guardrails, and evaluation harnesses. They serve it under cost and latency budgets that would be considered insane for a deterministic service. They debug failures that aren’t bugs in the conventional sense they’re distributional. The model is correct on average and wrong on the specific input the customer sent.

The job is real, the title is new, and the hardest part of doing it well is that the four roles above each look at it and say “that’s just my job with a wrapper.” It isn’t. It has its own discipline, and that discipline is what this book is.

Role Owns Asks Outputs
ML researcher Architectures, objectives What’s next? Papers, checkpoints
Data scientist Datasets, hypotheses What is true? Analyses, models
Backend engineer Services, infrastructure Will it stay up? Dashboards, APIs
AI engineer The system around the model Does it produce useful behavior at acceptable cost? Production AI features

If you do the job well, you read papers but don’t try to write them; you train models but mostly fine-tune; you build services but the service is half model and half retrieval and half prompt and half evaluation, and yes, that’s four halves, which is also part of the job.

The four things AI engineers actually do

Strip the buzzwords away and the work reduces to four loops.

1. Get the model to do the thing. Prompting, few-shot examples, structured outputs, function calling, retrieval, fine-tuning, alignment. This is the loop that takes a generic model and bends it to a specific purpose. It’s where most beginners spend most of their time, often longer than they should past a point, prompt engineering has diminishing returns and the leverage moves to retrieval and evals.

2. Know whether it’s doing the thing. Evaluation harnesses, golden datasets, LLM-as-a-judge, structured eval suites (RAGAS, DeepEval, G-Eval), human review queues, regression alerts. If you can’t tell whether a change made the model better or worse, you can’t make it better and ad-hoc “vibe checks” don’t survive contact with real users. The teams that ship reliable LLM features are the teams that took evals seriously early.

3. Run it at acceptable cost. Quantization, batching, caching, routing, autoscaling, choosing between hosted APIs and self-hosted serving, picking between vLLM and SGLang and TensorRT-LLM, knowing when prompt caching pays off, knowing when batch APIs at 50% off are good enough. Inference economics is its own subfield now and it has the property that small wrong assumptions compound into bills that get noticed. We give it most of Volume 3.

4. Keep it from doing harmful things. Guardrails, content filters, moderation classifiers, prompt-injection defenses, red-teaming, alignment-aware system design, regulatory compliance, model cards, watermarking, audit logs. Every feature you ship is a thing your model is now empowered to do, and every thing your model can do is a thing an adversary will try to abuse. This used to be optional. It is no longer optional.

These four loops are not phases. You run all four every week, on every system in production, forever. The book is organized around teaching them in the order they bite you, not in the order they’re most fashionable.

“From scratch” is not nostalgia

The title of this book includes the words from scratch, and people sometimes read that as a vanity flag an offer to make you reimplement things for the romance of it. It isn’t.

You will, in this book, write a tokenizer, a transformer, an attention mechanism, a training loop, a retrieval pipeline, an MCP server, an agent loop, and a small inference server. You will use them once and throw them away in the next chapter when we introduce the library that does it 100x faster.

The point is not the artifact. The point is that you can no longer be surprised by what’s inside the box. When PyTorch’s MultiheadAttention produces a NaN, you know which matmul is most likely to blame. When vLLM hits a KV-cache eviction storm, you have a mental model of what got evicted. When an agent loop spins forever, you’ve written one and you know which transitions are missing.

Calling the API is fine. Calling the API without ever having implemented what’s behind it leads to the kind of opaque, superstitious debugging that defines bad AI engineering. The boring default in this book is: build it once, then use the library for the rest of your career, and now you’ll know when the library is wrong.

This goes both ways. We will not pretend you should write your own transformer in production. You should not. The library is faster, more numerically stable, and tested by people whose job it is to test it. Throw your implementation away after the chapter ends it has done its job.

How the book is organized, in detail

Each chapter follows roughly the same shape:

  1. A scene or claim something concrete to anchor on.
  2. Why this exists what was missing before this idea, what it unlocked.
  3. The model the concept in prose, then code, then a real system.
  4. Sharp edges where it fails, what it costs, what it doesn’t tell you.
  5. What you’d actually use the production-ready library, the version that ships.
  6. What’s next one paragraph linking to the chapter that builds on this one.

You should read it linearly the first time. The volumes are independent in principle but the threads run through: a chapter on agents in Volume 2 references the transformer chapter in Volume 2, which references the math chapters in Volume 1, which assume the toolchain chapter is done. We mark prerequisites at the start of each chapter so you can skip with eyes open, but the assumption is linear, with skipping permitted, not random access.

Code blocks are runnable. The default language is Python with uv for environments we set this up in Chapter 2 and don’t relitigate it. TypeScript shows up in the agent and tools chapters; Rust appears in the inference chapters; Julia surfaces a few times when it’s genuinely the cleanest way to express the math. We tell you which language we’re switching to before we switch.

When code is shown for explanation rather than to be typed in, we say so. When code depends on the chapter’s setup, we name the setup. There are no comments inside code blocks explanations live in the prose around them, where they can be edited as the field changes.

Math appears when it pays for itself and is set in standard LaTeX. After every formula, one sentence in plain English about what it does, not what it is.

What you should already know

Mid-level software engineering. You’re comfortable with:

  • Python well enough to write a class, a function, and use a virtual environment.
  • Some flavor of typed language (TypeScript, Java, C++, Go, Rust) you have a sense of types even if you don’t always write them.
  • Git, the command line, HTTP, JSON, basic systems thinking. You can read a stack trace without panic.

You don’t need to:

  • Have a CS degree. Most of what’s in this book isn’t taught in undergrad CS.
  • Know any specific ML framework. We bring up PyTorch and JAX from zero in Volume 1.
  • Have used GPUs. We assume you’ll get one (locally, in Colab, or on a cloud) and walk through it in Chapter 2.
  • Know any specific paper. We cite when it matters and explain in our own words.

If you’ve already shipped LLM features, you can skim Volume 1 for the math chapters you don’t already have intuition on, and start in earnest at Volume 2. If you’re brand new to AI engineering but a strong engineer in another domain, start at Chapter 2.

The companion curriculum

This book grew out of a curriculum: 20 phases, 380+ lessons, the kind of structured learning path most engineers wish existed but rarely commit to building. The curriculum lives at /phases/ in the book’s source repository. It’s where the source material for every chapter came from.

You don’t need the curriculum to read the book the book is self-contained. But if a chapter intrigues you and you want more code, more exercises, more depth on a single technique, the corresponding phase is one folder away. Each chapter notes its source phases in passing, the way a paper notes related work. We don’t quote the lessons, but they’re a fine companion.

What “modern” means in this book

This is a snapshot. The book is written in 2026 and its examples reflect the field as of late 2025 / early 2026: Claude 4.x, GPT-5, DeepSeek-V3, Llama 4, Stable Diffusion 3, MCP as the default tool-calling protocol, vLLM and SGLang as the dominant open serving stacks, A2A as the inter-agent protocol, EU AI Act enforcement underway, the post-RLHF alignment toolkit (DPO and friends) firmly mainstream.

By the time you read this, some of those will be wrong. We mark when we’re describing a snapshot and when we’re describing a principle. The snapshots will rot; the principles won’t. The principle that prompt caching pays back when prompts have a stable prefix will outlive the specific TTL on Anthropic’s prompt cache. The principle that mixing autoregressive and diffusion in one architecture lets you use the strengths of each will outlive Transfusion. The principle that an agent without checkpoints is a nightly news segment waiting to happen will outlive whatever this year’s durable-execution library is called.

We don’t apologize for the snapshot dating. A book that refuses to mention specifics is a book that doesn’t say anything.

Where we go next

Chapter 2 sets up the toolchain. It is the most boring chapter in the book and the most important to actually do. Skip it and the next twenty chapters will be a fight against your environment instead of a fight to learn something. We’ll install Python with uv, get a GPU working (or arrange access to one), set up Docker, configure your editor, and write the verification script that confirms everything is wired together.

Then we go to the math. Three chapters, in Volume 1: linear algebra (the chapter you’ll come back to most), calculus and probability (the chapter most engineers wish someone had taught them like this), and the rest statistics, sampling, spectral methods, the toolkit that makes the rest of the book make sense.

After that, classical ML, deep learning, vision, language, audio. Then in Volume 2 we get to the transformer, and from the transformer the rest of modern AI unfolds in the order it was discovered.

Open the next chapter when you’re ready.

Chapter 2 Setting Up the Toolchain

There is a specific kind of failure that happens around 11pm on a Tuesday. You are halfway through a tutorial. You paste the install command. The terminal scrolls for a minute, looks fine, and then five lines from the bottom drops a ResolutionImpossible error referencing four packages you have never heard of. You scroll back up. You install something else. The new install uninstalls the thing the tutorial actually needed. You try pip install --force-reinstall. Now import torch works but reports a CUDA version that does not match your driver. By midnight you have closed the tutorial, opened Colab, and concluded that AI is mostly a YAML problem.

This chapter is about not doing that.

The work in the rest of this book fitting models, writing tokenizers, calling agents, serving inference assumes a working machine. Working means you can install a package without thinking, your GPU is reachable from Python, your editor talks to your environment, your secrets are not in your repo, and when something breaks you can tell which layer broke. We are going to build that machine once, on purpose, and we are going to spend a chapter doing it because every chapter after this assumes it is done.

Why this is the most boring chapter and the one most worth doing

Most engineers approach setup as throat-clearing. The exciting part the model, the training run, the agent loop is on the other side of the install commands, so the install commands get rushed. The install commands are also where the field is least standardized. Different operating systems, different GPUs, different Python versions, different CUDA versions, different package managers, different shells all of it interacts, and most of the interactions are subtle, undocumented, and version-sensitive.

The result is that environment problems are the most common reason engineers stall on AI projects, and they are also the least interesting bugs to debug. Nothing about a CUDA version mismatch teaches you anything about machine learning. It just costs you a Saturday.

A clean toolchain is not glamour. It is leverage. It compounds across hundreds of future install commands, hundreds of future debugging sessions, every code review, every notebook you open, every time you hand a teammate a clone-and-run repo and it works. Skip this chapter and you will pay it back in fragments forever.

The four-layer stack

Every working AI engineering setup is a stack of four layers. They depend on each other from the bottom up, and they have to be installed in order or nothing works.

Layer 4: AI/ML libraries     PyTorch, JAX, transformers, diffusers, vLLM, ...
Layer 3: Language runtimes   Python 3.12, Node 22, Rust 1.80+, occasionally Julia
Layer 2: Package managers    uv (Python), pnpm (JS/TS), cargo (Rust), juliaup (Julia)
Layer 1: System foundation   OS, shell, git, editor, GPU drivers, CUDA toolkit

When something breaks, the first question is always: which layer is wrong? PyTorch can’t see the GPU? That’s almost always Layer 1 (driver) or Layer 4 (PyTorch built for a different CUDA). uv can’t find Python? Layer 2 versus Layer 3. Your training script imports a package the linter doesn’t see? Your editor isn’t pointed at the right Layer 3. Once you have this map, debugging environment problems goes from “magic incantations from Stack Overflow” to a methodical walk down the stack.

We install layer 1 first, then 2, then 3, then 4 never the other way around and we install each layer the same way on every machine you’ll ever own. The boring default is: same package managers, same Python version pinned per project, same shell, same editor config. Variety in your toolchain costs you compounding interest.

System foundation

Pick one operating system and stop apologizing for it.

The reality of 2026 is that AI engineering is built on Linux first, macOS second, Windows third through WSL2. The frontier-lab serving stacks (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM), the GPU drivers, the kernel hackery for paged attention, the inference servers all of it ships and tests on Linux. Your dev machine doesn’t have to be Linux, but the abstractions you’ll work against assume Linux underneath.

For a development laptop you have three reasonable choices:

  • Linux (Ubuntu 24.04 LTS or Debian 12 are the boring defaults). Maximum compatibility with everything else in this book. Slightly more friction for the laptop-as-a-laptop experience, but if you spend most of your time at a terminal anyway, the friction disappears in a week.
  • macOS with Apple Silicon. Excellent developer ergonomics. CPU and unified memory are surprisingly competitive for inference of small models via MPS (Metal Performance Shaders). For training or serving anything serious, you will rent a GPU box anyway.
  • Windows with WSL2 running Ubuntu. WSL2 is a real Linux kernel; Docker, GPU passthrough (with NVIDIA’s WSL drivers), and most of the toolchain in this book work as if you were on native Linux. Treat the Windows side as a thin shell that hosts the Linux side.

You do not need to switch operating systems for this book but inside whatever OS you have, you will live in a POSIX shell (bash or zsh), and the commands in this book assume that shell. PowerShell is a fine system shell; it’s not the AI engineering shell. Run the AI work from inside WSL or a terminal multiplexer that talks to Linux.

The system-foundation install is short:

# Ubuntu / Debian
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y build-essential git curl wget ca-certificates

# macOS
xcode-select --install
brew install git curl wget

# Windows (one-time, from PowerShell as Administrator)
wsl --install -d Ubuntu-24.04
# Then continue inside WSL as if it were Ubuntu.

Three things matter here: a C/C++ toolchain (because some Python packages compile native extensions), git (because everything else assumes it), and a working SSL stack (because everything you install is over HTTPS).

Git, configured once

You will use git on every machine you touch for the rest of your career. Configure it now, globally, the same way every time, and never think about it again.

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
git config --global pull.rebase true
git config --global core.autocrlf input    # macOS/Linux
git config --global core.editor "code --wait"   # or your editor of choice

Generate an SSH key, add it to GitHub (and any other forge you use), and stop typing your password:

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@example.com"
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
# paste into GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys

There are heavier git practices signed commits, multiple identities, GPG keys for verified releases and we’ll come back to them when they matter. For now, you have a name, an email, a default branch, and an SSH key. That is enough to clone, branch, commit, push, and pull-request your way through the rest of this book.

The one durable rule of git for this book: branch early, commit often, push every day. AI projects produce a lot of accidentally-good and accidentally-bad results, and you want a graph that lets you find your way back to the version that worked.

Python with uv

Almost everything in this book is Python, so the Python toolchain is where most of the leverage lives.

The boring default for Python in 2026 is uv, a single Rust-based binary that handles four jobs that used to need four tools: installing Python itself, creating virtual environments, installing packages, and resolving lockfiles. It is 10–100x faster than pip, has the most reliable resolver of the lot, and unifies the workflow across machines.

Install it once:

curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
# or, on Windows from PowerShell:
# powershell -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"

Install Python through uv instead of through your OS:

uv python install 3.12
uv python list

That second command shows every Python uv knows about and which one is current. We standardize on Python 3.12 for this book. 3.13 is fine where supported, but a few of the AI/ML libraries in Volume 2 lag a release behind on the bleeding-edge Python, and 3.12 is the version where everything currently works.

Now create a project. The shape of a Python project for the rest of this book is:

my-project/
├── pyproject.toml          declarative project config
├── uv.lock                 pinned, reproducible dependency graph
├── .venv/                  virtual environment (gitignored)
├── .python-version         pins Python version per project
├── README.md
├── src/
│   └── my_project/
│       └── __init__.py
└── tests/
    └── test_smoke.py

You do not have to type that out. uv makes it for you:

uv init my-project
cd my-project
uv add numpy matplotlib jupyter
uv run python -c "import numpy; print(numpy.__version__)"

Three things just happened. uv init wrote a pyproject.toml, a .python-version, and a stub package. uv add resolved your dependencies, wrote them to pyproject.toml, created the virtual environment if it didn’t exist, installed the packages, and updated the lockfile. uv run ran a command inside that environment without you having to manually activate it.

This last bit deserves emphasis. The traditional Python flow has you source .venv/bin/activate, hope you remember to deactivate when you switch projects, and silently install into the wrong environment when you forget. With uv, you do not activate. You prefix commands with uv run and the right environment is used. If you prefer the activation flow, source .venv/bin/activate still works. But the activation-free flow is more reliable across editors, terminal sessions, and CI.

A real pyproject.toml for an early chapter of this book looks like:

[project]
name = "ai-engineering-foundations"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
dependencies = [
    "numpy>=1.26",
    "matplotlib>=3.8",
    "scipy>=1.13",
    "scikit-learn>=1.4",
    "jupyterlab>=4.2",
    "ipykernel>=6.29",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
torch = ["torch>=2.4", "torchvision>=0.19"]
llm   = ["anthropic>=0.39", "openai>=1.50"]

[tool.uv]
dev-dependencies = [
    "pytest>=8.0",
    "ruff>=0.5",
    "mypy>=1.10",
]

Optional dependency groups are how you keep your environments lean. The math chapters of Volume 1 don’t need PyTorch; the deep-learning chapters do. The LLM chapters in Volume 2 need the Anthropic and OpenAI SDKs; the math chapters don’t. You install only what the work needs:

uv sync                       # base dependencies
uv sync --extra torch         # base + PyTorch
uv sync --extra torch --extra llm

uv sync is the command you’ll type most. It installs exactly what’s pinned in the lockfile and nothing else, deletes packages you’ve removed from pyproject.toml, and produces an environment that is byte-for-byte the same as your collaborator’s.

Lockfiles and reproducibility

A pyproject.toml says what you depend on. A uv.lock says exactly which versions of every direct and transitive dependency the resolver picked. The lockfile is what makes “clone the repo, run uv sync, get the same environment” actually true.

Commit uv.lock to git. Always. The cost is a few hundred kilobytes; the benefit is that nine months from now, when a transitive dependency has had a breaking release, your old project still installs.

echo ".venv/" >> .gitignore
echo "*.egg-info/" >> .gitignore
echo "__pycache__/" >> .gitignore

What you commit: pyproject.toml, uv.lock, .python-version, your source. What you do not commit: the virtual environment itself.

When uv is not the right answer

uv is the boring default. There are two cases where you reach for something else.

The first is conda, when you need non-Python system libraries managed alongside Python a specific CUDA toolkit, a specific cuDNN, a specific NCCL, on a shared cluster where you can’t apt install anything. Use Miniconda (not the full Anaconda distribution; you don’t need its 3GB of preinstalled packages), keep one rule sacred don’t mix pip install and conda install in the same environment and treat conda as a managed layer below uv, not a competitor to it.

The second is plain venv, the standard library’s built-in. It’s slower than uv, has no lockfile, and forces you back into the activation dance. But it ships with every Python, so on a stranger’s machine where you can’t install uv, python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt is always available. Treat it as the fallback, not the workflow.

The other languages

Most chapters are Python. A few are not.

TypeScript appears in Volume 2’s tools, agents, and MCP server chapters, and in Volume 3 for some serving and ops tooling. The boring default is Node 22 LTS managed through fnm (a fast Rust-based Node version manager), with pnpm as the package manager.

curl -fsSL https://fnm.vercel.app/install | bash
fnm install 22 && fnm use 22
npm install -g pnpm
pnpm --version

pnpm is faster than npm and stricter about hoisting, which catches a class of “works on my machine” bugs early. If your team uses npm or yarn, use what your team uses; the differences are small. If you have no constraint, use pnpm.

Rust appears in inference and infra chapters in Volume 3, and once or twice in Volume 2 when we’re showing the source side of a tool that’s written in Rust. Install it through rustup, which lets you have multiple toolchains side by side:

curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
source $HOME/.cargo/env
rustc --version

Rust’s package manager is cargo. It comes with the toolchain. There is no uv-like alternative because cargo is already that good.

Julia appears occasionally in Volume 1’s math chapters when it’s genuinely the cleanest way to express something particularly in Chapter 5 where its array semantics make spectral methods read like the math. Install through juliaup:

curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh
julia -e 'println("Julia ", VERSION)'

You can skip Julia until a chapter calls for it. We tell you when it does.

You will not need C++, Go, or any other language to follow this book. We name them when they’re under the hood vLLM is largely Python with C++/CUDA kernels; TensorRT-LLM has a heavy C++ core; Triton is its own language for kernel writing. None of those require you to write or compile them yourself.

GPU setup

The truth about GPUs in 2026: you do not need to own one to get through Volume 1, and you should not buy one for Volume 2 unless you already know exactly what you’d do with it.

Here is the matrix.

Need Hardware Cost When to use
Run notebook code on small models CPU only Free, already yours Volume 1 most of the way through
Inference of 7B–13B models, light fine-tuning Consumer GPU (RTX 4090, 24GB) or rented A10 $0.50–$1.50/hr rented Volume 2 LLM chapters at small scale
Fine-tuning 7B+, serious training Rented A100 80GB or H100 $1.50–$3.50/hr rented Volume 2 fine-tuning, serving chapters
Multi-node training H100 cluster $$$ Frontier work; mostly out of scope here

For most readers, the answer is: don’t buy hardware. Rent it when you need it. Modal, RunPod, Lambda Labs, Vast.ai, and the major clouds (AWS, GCP, Azure) all rent GPUs by the hour. A few hours of an A100 costs less than dinner. The argument for buying a 4090 is real if you have a steady stream of small training jobs and value the latency of “click and go”, but for a book reader it almost never pencils out.

If you do have an NVIDIA GPU, the install is:

# Check the driver
nvidia-smi

# Install PyTorch with CUDA support, matching your driver's CUDA version
uv pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu124

nvidia-smi reports a “CUDA Version” in its top-right corner. That’s the maximum CUDA version your driver supports, not the version you have installed. PyTorch needs a CUDA version that’s less than or equal to that. The cu124 in the URL above is asking for the PyTorch build compiled against CUDA 12.4. If your driver reports 12.4 or higher, that wheel is fine.

Verify:

import torch
print("CUDA available:", torch.cuda.is_available())
print("Device count:", torch.cuda.device_count())
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    print("Device 0:", torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))
    x = torch.randn(1024, 1024, device="cuda")
    y = x @ x.T
    print("Tensor on", y.device, "shape", tuple(y.shape))

If cuda.is_available() is False, the driver and the PyTorch build don’t match. The fix is one of: install a different PyTorch build (different cuXXX URL), update your driver, or stop using a Docker image that masked the GPU.

If you are on Apple Silicon, you don’t have CUDA. You have MPS Apple’s Metal Performance Shaders backend in PyTorch. It is slower than a recent NVIDIA GPU but real, and good enough for Volume 1 and small Volume 2 work:

import torch
print("MPS available:", torch.backends.mps.is_available())
device = torch.device("mps" if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else "cpu")
x = torch.randn(1024, 1024, device=device)
y = x @ x.T

Some PyTorch operators don’t yet have MPS implementations. When you hit one, set PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK=1 and PyTorch will silently fall back to CPU for that op. Slower, but it runs.

For rented GPUs, the workflow is the same as a local Linux box, with two differences. First, you usually get an SSH endpoint and a Jupyter URL pick whichever you prefer; this book is friendly to both. Second, you should treat the box as ephemeral: keep your code in git, your data in object storage, your secrets in a secrets manager, and assume the disk vanishes when the rental ends. We come back to this in Volume 3 when we serve at scale; for now, just don’t store anything on a GPU rental that you can’t reproduce.

For Google Colab, the boring default is the free tier for tinkering and Colab Pro for occasional bigger work. Mount Google Drive for persistence, install dependencies into the runtime each session (Colab does not let you persist environments cleanly), and accept that the runtime will time out if you walk away. Colab is excellent for a tutorial, mediocre for a serious project, and bad for anything you can’t fully reproduce from a fresh runtime.

Renting a GPU box, end to end

Most readers will rent before they buy, so it’s worth walking the workflow once.

The choice is between on-demand GPUs (you pay for the time you have them, the box is yours, it doesn’t go away) and spot or interruptible GPUs (cheaper often 50–80% off but the provider can reclaim them with a few minutes of notice). For interactive work, use on-demand and stop the box when you walk away. For long training runs, spot only if your training loop checkpoints frequently and resumes from checkpoint cleanly. We’ll come back to checkpoint discipline in Volume 2.

The mainstream rental options in 2026, ranked by how friendly they are for an individual reader:

  • Modal and RunPod best ergonomics for “I want a GPU notebook in 60 seconds and I don’t want to think about VPCs.” Modal in particular has a Python-native API where your local function runs remotely with one decorator; it’s the boring default for “I have a script and I want it to run on an H100.” RunPod gives you a more traditional SSH-able box.
  • Lambda Labs and Vast.ai competitive on price for raw machines, slightly more friction. Vast.ai is a marketplace of community GPUs; quality varies, prices are excellent.
  • AWS EC2 / GCP / Azure full clouds. Worth learning if you’ll deploy at work. Heavier setup; you’ll spend an afternoon on IAM and VPCs before your first training step.

A reader-friendly rental flow looks like this. Pick a provider. Create an SSH key on your laptop (or use the one you generated earlier in this chapter). Paste the public key into the provider’s console. Launch an A10 or A100 instance with a base image that already has CUDA and a recent PyTorch every provider offers one. SSH in. Clone your project. uv sync. Run your training script. When it’s done, stop the instance.

# On your laptop
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 ubuntu@<rental-ip>

# On the rental box
git clone git@github.com:you/my-project.git
cd my-project
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
source $HOME/.local/bin/env
uv sync --extra torch
uv run python train.py

The mistake to avoid is treating a rental like your laptop. Don’t pip install outside a venv. Don’t drop datasets onto the rental’s local disk and forget about them when the rental ends, they’re gone. Move artifacts to object storage as part of your training script, not as an afterthought:

import boto3, pathlib

def upload_checkpoint(local: pathlib.Path, s3_uri: str):
    bucket, _, key = s3_uri.removeprefix("s3://").partition("/")
    boto3.client("s3").upload_file(str(local), bucket, key)

For long-lived work, attach a persistent volume the provider keeps even when the instance is stopped. Modal’s volumes, RunPod’s network storage, AWS EBS same pattern. Treat the GPU as ephemeral, the volume as semi-persistent, and your real source of truth as git plus object storage.

Docker

You don’t have to use Docker for this book. You should anyway.

Docker is the boring default for “this code, this environment, run on any machine” once you outgrow uv sync. It packages your application and every dependency the Python version, the system libraries, the CUDA runtime into a single image, and runs it the same way on your laptop, on a teammate’s laptop, in CI, and in production.

For AI work specifically, Docker matters in three places:

  1. CUDA-pinned environments that don’t survive the host upgrading. An image pinned to nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-cudnn-runtime-ubuntu22.04 keeps working forever.
  2. Inference servers in Volume 3, where the deployable artifact is a container.
  3. Reproducing other people’s work papers and open-source projects increasingly ship a Dockerfile as the canonical way to run their code.

Install Docker Desktop on macOS or Windows; Docker Engine on Linux. For GPU access on Linux you also install the NVIDIA Container Toolkit:

distribution=$(. /etc/os-release; echo $ID$VERSION_ID)
curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg
curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/$distribution/libnvidia-container.list \
  | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart docker

Verify:

docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi

That should print the same nvidia-smi output as on the host. The --gpus all flag is the magic that maps the host GPUs into the container.

A simple AI Dockerfile follows the multi-stage build pattern: one stage installs and compiles, a second stage carries only what’s needed at runtime, and the runtime image is small and boring.

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.7

FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
ENV UV_LINK_MODE=copy
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir uv
WORKDIR /app
COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./
RUN uv sync --frozen --no-dev
COPY src ./src

FROM python:3.12-slim AS runtime
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app /app
ENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"
CMD ["python", "-m", "src.my_project"]

Three things to notice. First, --frozen tells uv to fail rather than re-resolve, which is what you want in CI your image must reflect the lockfile or fail loudly. Second, separating dependency install (COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock then uv sync) from source copy (COPY src) means changing your code doesn’t bust Docker’s cache for the slow dependency install step. Third, the runtime stage carries only the compiled environment, not the build tools smaller, more secure, and faster to push and pull.

For GPU images, swap the base for a CUDA-enabled one (nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-cudnn-runtime-ubuntu22.04) and add Python on top. We’ll write the full version of this in Volume 3 when we deploy a real inference server.

The one rule of Docker for AI projects: never build a 30GB image because you put your dataset in it. Datasets live in object storage or mounted volumes; images are for code and environments.

Editor and shell

Your editor and shell are where you spend the rest of your career. Pick once, configure deeply, sync across machines.

For the editor, the boring default in 2026 is VS Code or its AI-first fork Cursor. Both are Electron-based, both speak the same extension API, and the Python ecosystem (Pylance, Ruff, debugpy, Jupyter) is best-supported there. JetBrains’ PyCharm is excellent for pure-Python projects with a strong type story; Vim and Neovim are excellent for the people who already use them. The wrong move is to spend a week shopping for editors instead of writing code.

The two settings that matter most:

{
  "python.defaultInterpreterPath": "${workspaceFolder}/.venv/bin/python",
  "python.terminal.activateEnvironment": true,
  "[python]": {
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "charliermarsh.ruff"
  },
  "files.exclude": {
    "**/__pycache__": true,
    "**/.pytest_cache": true,
    "**/.mypy_cache": true
  }
}

The first line points your editor at the project’s virtual environment so import resolution works without you doing anything. The second formats on save with Ruff, which replaces black, isort, and flake8 with one Rust-based tool that’s roughly 100x faster. The exclude block keeps your file tree readable.

For the shell, bash and zsh are both fine. Pick one, customize it lightly, and stop tweaking. Two settings that pay back:

# In ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc

# Always show which virtual environment is active
PS1='$(if [ -n "$VIRTUAL_ENV" ]; then echo "($(basename $VIRTUAL_ENV)) "; fi)'$PS1

# Quick activation helper
alias venv='source .venv/bin/activate'

# uv shortcuts
alias uvr='uv run'
alias uvs='uv sync'

The fancy version of “shell + prompt + history search + completions” is Starship + zsh + fzf + zoxide. The simple version is what your distro shipped with. Either is fine. The trap to avoid is spending a weekend on dotfiles when you should be reading Chapter 3.

Notebooks: when, when not, and how

You will use Jupyter notebooks in this book. You should also know when to throw them away.

Notebooks are good for: exploration, EDA, plotting intermediate results, sharing a self-contained narrative with someone who doesn’t want to clone your repo. They are bad for: production code, code that needs to be tested, code that runs in a pipeline, and any module that another module wants to import.

The discipline is: notebooks are for exploring. As soon as the code you’re writing in the notebook needs to be reused by you, by a teammate, by a script you move it into a .py file under src/, import it back into the notebook, and continue exploring with the imported version.

Set up Jupyter with uv:

uv add --dev jupyterlab ipykernel
uv run python -m ipykernel install --user --name my-project --display-name "Python (my-project)"
uv run jupyter lab

Two practices that make notebooks survive into next month:

  • Restart and run all before you commit a notebook. If it doesn’t run top-to-bottom, it’s lying about its state.
  • Strip outputs from version control. The nbstripout package, hooked into git, removes cell outputs before commit so your diffs are reviewable and your repo doesn’t carry megabytes of plot images.
uv add --dev nbstripout
uv run nbstripout --install

We’ll come back to notebooks several times in Volume 1, especially in the math chapters, where they’re the natural unit of explanation. Just remember: a notebook is a good explainer; it is a bad library.

Secrets and APIs

Every chapter from Volume 2 onward calls APIs. Your environment needs to know how to reach them; your repository must not.

The boring default is .env files plus a loader, with the file gitignored.

# .env at the project root (NEVER commit this)
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
HF_TOKEN=hf_...

from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os

load_dotenv()
api_key = os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]

Add .env to .gitignore before you ever put a real key in it. Then commit a .env.example with the variable names but no values, so collaborators know which keys to set:

# .env.example (safe to commit)
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=
OPENAI_API_KEY=
HF_TOKEN=

For anything that touches production even your own side projects graduate to a real secrets manager: 1Password’s CLI, AWS Secrets Manager, Doppler, Infisical, HashiCorp Vault. The pattern is the same: secrets live in the manager, your code reads them through an SDK or a CLI that injects them as env vars at runtime, and the secret never touches a file on disk that could end up in git or in a backup.

Two failure modes to internalize early:

  1. Committing a key by accident is one of the most common security incidents in AI projects. Rotate it the moment you notice. GitHub’s secret scanner will email you within minutes; assume your key is exfiltrated by then.
  2. Logging the key to stdout during debugging is the second most common. Never print(os.environ). If you have to log config, redact the secret-looking fields.

We’ll go deeper into secrets when we ship things in Volume 3. For Volumes 1 and 2, .env plus discipline is enough.

Data management

AI projects accumulate data fast. A 200MB CSV becomes a 4GB Parquet becomes a 40GB image dataset becomes a checkpoint directory you can’t fit on your laptop. Know how you’re going to handle this before it bites you.

The boring rules:

  • Code in git, data outside git. Datasets, model weights, generated outputs, and large logs do not belong in your code repository. They make clones slow and pushes painful, and GitHub will block files larger than 100MB anyway.
  • Use object storage for shared datasets. S3, GCS, R2, MinIO pick one, learn its CLI, and stop emailing zip files. For solo work, an S3 bucket plus the aws s3 cp CLI is fine.
  • Track datasets with DVC or git-lfs when you need versioning. DVC stores pointers in git and the actual blobs in object storage; git-lfs is simpler but charges per gigabyte at GitHub.
  • Cache model weights in a known location (HF_HOME=~/.cache/huggingface) so the same 30GB checkpoint isn’t downloaded once per project.

A small data/ discipline that survives:

my-project/
├── data/                 (gitignored)
│   ├── raw/              ← never edit; canonical source data
│   ├── interim/          ← intermediate results from notebooks
│   └── processed/        ← model-ready datasets
├── outputs/              (gitignored)
│   ├── checkpoints/
│   └── runs/
└── src/...

Treat data/raw/ as immutable. If you transformed it, the result goes in interim/ or processed/, never on top of the raw file. This is the single rule that saves the most “wait, what did I actually train on” debugging.

Debugging and profiling

When something is wrong, you need to know which layer is wrong, and you need a tool that can see into it. The boring default toolkit:

  • pdb / ipdb for interactive debugging in scripts and notebooks. breakpoint() in modern Python drops you in.
  • pytest -x --pdb to drop into the debugger on the first failing test.
  • py-spy for sampling profilers without changing your code: py-spy top --pid <pid> shows where a Python process is spending its time, live.
  • scalene for combined CPU and memory profiling with a clean report.
  • nvtop / nvidia-smi --loop=1 for live GPU utilization.
  • NVIDIA Nsight Systems (nsys) for serious GPU/CPU traces, especially for understanding training-loop and inference bottlenecks. Heavy, but unmatched when you actually need it.
  • viztracer for fast end-to-end traces of a Python program.

You don’t need to install all of these today. Install ipdb and py-spy now, and add the others when a chapter calls for them.

A worked example you’ll do many times: a training step is unexpectedly slow.

# In one terminal, find the PID of your training script
ps aux | grep train.py

# In another, attach py-spy
py-spy top --pid 12345

If 60% of the time is in forward, the model is the cost. If 60% is in DataLoader, the data pipeline is the cost. If 60% is in optimizer.step, the optimizer or the gradient sync is the cost. Each of those is a different fix in a different chapter, and the profiler is what tells you which one.

For GPU-side bottlenecks, the equivalent diagnosis is “is the GPU pegged?” nvidia-smi --loop=1 while the job runs. If utilization is below 50%, the bottleneck is on the CPU side feeding the GPU. If utilization is at 99% and your step is still slow, the model itself is the bottleneck. We’ll come back to this in Volume 2 when training loops and serving stacks become the subject.

Sharp edges

Things that will break for you, in roughly the order they break.

Path vs. shell mismatch. You install something with one shell open and try to use it in another. Restart the shell, or source ~/.bashrc. If a binary really isn’t on PATH, which <tool> is the first thing to check.

The wrong Python. python resolves to system Python (or no Python on macOS, by default), not your project’s Python. Use uv run python or activate the venv. which python should print a path inside .venv/.

CUDA mismatch. nvidia-smi says one thing, python -c "import torch; print(torch.version.cuda)" says another. The PyTorch CUDA version must be ≤ the driver’s reported CUDA version. Reinstall PyTorch with the right cu1XX URL.

Out of memory. GPU OOM is the most common failure in Volume 2. Reduce batch size, enable gradient accumulation, or move to a bigger GPU. We cover the techniques in Chapter 17 (Building an LLM); for now, smaller batches.

Locale and encoding. Some tutorials assume UTF-8 everywhere. Windows historically did not. Inside WSL or Linux you’re fine; on bare Windows, set PYTHONUTF8=1 in your environment.

Permission errors on Linux. pip install without --user or without a venv tries to write to system directories and fails. The fix is the venv, not sudo. Never sudo pip install.

Docker can’t see the GPU. Either you didn’t pass --gpus all, or you skipped the NVIDIA Container Toolkit step, or you’re on Docker Desktop without WSL2 GPU support enabled. The docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.4.1-base-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi smoke test catches all three.

Mixed package managers. pip install inside a conda env, brew install of something uv already manages, npm install -g of something fnm should manage. Pick one tool per layer and stick to it.

Notebook state lying. A cell ran an hour ago, you deleted it, the variable it created still exists in memory. Restart Kernel is the answer to half of “but it worked five minutes ago.”

What you’d actually use

A summary of the boring defaults, by job:

Job Tool Why
OS Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, macOS, or Windows + WSL2 Ubuntu Linux compat, ergonomic dev experience
Shell bash or zsh POSIX, ubiquitous
Python install uv python install Pinned per project, reproducible
Python env + packages uv (uv add, uv sync, uv run) Fast, lockfile-native, single tool
Python project config pyproject.toml + uv.lock Standardized, reproducible
JS/TS env fnm + pnpm Versioned Node, strict deps
Rust env rustup + cargo One-stop, official
Editor VS Code or Cursor Best Python tooling, AI-native fork available
Linting/formatting ruff + mypy Single fast linter, real type checker
Notebooks JupyterLab + ipykernel per project Project-local kernels
Secrets .env in dev, secrets manager in prod Out of git, in process env
Containers Docker + NVIDIA Container Toolkit Reproducible runtime, GPU passthrough
GPU access Local NVIDIA / Apple MPS / rented (Modal, RunPod, Lambda) Pay for what you use
Datasets Object storage + DVC Code in git, data outside
Debugging ipdb, py-spy, nvitop, nsys Layer-aware tools

If you forget everything else in this chapter, remember: install Python through uv, manage projects with uv, commit your uv.lock, keep secrets in .env, run GPUs through Docker with the NVIDIA Container Toolkit, and never put data in git. Those six rules cover most of the failure modes the next thirty-five chapters will throw at you.

A verification script

End every setup session by running the script that proves it worked. Save the following to verify_setup.py at the root of your project:

import importlib
import shutil
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path

def check(name, fn):
    try:
        result = fn()
        print(f"[OK]   {name}: {result}")
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"[FAIL] {name}: {e}")
        return False

def cmd_version(cmd, *args):
    if shutil.which(cmd) is None:
        raise RuntimeError(f"{cmd} not on PATH")
    out = subprocess.run([cmd, *args], capture_output=True, text=True, check=True)
    return (out.stdout or out.stderr).strip().splitlines()[0]

ok = True
ok &= check("Python version", lambda: sys.version.split()[0])
ok &= check("Inside venv", lambda: str(Path(sys.executable).resolve()))
ok &= check("uv", lambda: cmd_version("uv", "--version"))
ok &= check("git", lambda: cmd_version("git", "--version"))
ok &= check("docker", lambda: cmd_version("docker", "--version"))
ok &= check("numpy", lambda: importlib.import_module("numpy").__version__)

try:
    import torch
    ok &= check("torch", lambda: torch.__version__)
    if torch.cuda.is_available():
        ok &= check("CUDA device", lambda: torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))
    elif torch.backends.mps.is_available():
        ok &= check("MPS", lambda: "available")
    else:
        print("[WARN] No GPU acceleration (CPU only)")
except ImportError:
    print("[WARN] torch not installed yet (fine for early chapters)")

sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)

Run it from inside your project’s environment:

uv run python verify_setup.py

If everything prints [OK], the rest of the book will run. If something prints [FAIL], fix it before moving on. The script is intentionally short extend it with the things specific to your machine, but keep it as the smoke test you run after every reinstall.

Where this goes next

The next three chapters are the math. Linear algebra (Chapter 3), the chapter you’ll come back to most as the rest of the book unfolds; calculus and probability (Chapter 4), where the engineering of learning gets honest; statistics, sampling, and spectral methods (Chapter 5), the toolkit that makes the chapters after it make sense.

If you’ve kept this chapter’s setup honest, you can read those three with a notebook open beside the book, run the code blocks as you go, and treat each formula as something you can verify numerically by hand. That’s the way Volume 1 is meant to be read not as theory you accept, but as theory you check, in the environment you just built.

Open the next chapter when you’re ready.

Chapter 3 Linear Algebra You’ll Actually Use

You are three weeks into building a retrieval pipeline. The embeddings look right. The cosine similarity scores look right. Then you notice the top result for “how do I reset my password” is a page about office plants. You open the debugger. The embeddings are 768-dimensional vectors. The similarity is a dot product. Somewhere in those 768 numbers, the model has decided that “password” and “philodendron” are neighbors. The bug is not in your Python. The bug is in the geometry of the space.

Linear algebra is not the thing you study to pass a math exam. It is the operating system your model runs on. Every forward pass is a sequence of matrix multiplications. Every similarity search is a dot product. Every token the model predicts is a vector that was pulled from an embedding table, projected through attention scores, and transformed by weight matrices. When the model produces nonsense, the nonsense lives in the geometry of those transformations. This chapter gives you the vocabulary to debug it.

Why This Exists

Before neural networks, linear algebra was a tool for graphics programmers and control systems engineers. You learned it to rotate 3D models and solve circuit equations. The AI era changed the value proposition. Now every layer of every model is a matrix, every prediction is a vector, and every training step is a composition of linear and nonlinear operations. The field inverted: what used to be the most abstract branch of applied math became the most concrete thing in your stack.

Chapter 2 gave you a working machine. Now you need the language to read what that machine is computing. The next three chapters are the math linear algebra, calculus and probability, statistics and spectral methods but they are not three separate subjects. They are three views of the same thing: a model’s behavior. Linear algebra is the view of what the model does. Calculus is the view of how it learns to do it. Probability is the view of what it means when it says it’s done. Here, we start with what it does.

A Vector Is a Point in Your Model’s Universe

A vector is a list of numbers. The list has a length, and each position in the list is a dimension. In 2D you write [3, 2] and picture an arrow from the origin to the point (3, 2). In AI, a vector has hundreds or thousands of numbers, and you cannot picture it. But the rules that govern two dimensions govern all of them.

In this book, vectors represent everything:

  • A word is a vector. When you embed the word “king” with an embedding model, you get something like 768 floating-point numbers. Those numbers are coordinates in a space where “king” is close to “queen” and far from “cabbage.”
  • An image is a vector. Flatten the RGB values of a 224x224 photo and you have a 150,528-dimensional vector. Every pixel is a dimension.
  • A user’s preferences are a vector. A recommendation system maps users to points in a preference space, then finds neighbors.

The three operations you do with vectors are addition, scalar multiplication, and the dot product. Addition moves a point. Scalar multiplication stretches it. The dot product measures how similar two points are.

class Vector:
    def __init__(self, components):
        self.components = list(components)

    def __add__(self, other):
        return Vector([a + b for a, b in zip(self.components, other.components)])

    def __sub__(self, other):
        return Vector([a - b for a, b in zip(self.components, other.components)])

    def dot(self, other):
        return sum(a * b for a, b in zip(self.components, other.components))

    def magnitude(self):
        return sum(x**2 for x in self.components) ** 0.5

    def normalize(self):
        mag = self.magnitude()
        return Vector([x / mag for x in self.components])

    def cosine_similarity(self, other):
        return self.dot(other) / (self.magnitude() * other.magnitude())

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Vector({self.components})"


a = Vector([1, 2, 3])
b = Vector([4, 5, 6])

print(f"a + b = {a + b}")
print(f"dot(a, b) = {a.dot(b)}")
print(f"cos(a, b) = {a.cosine_similarity(b):.4f}")

The implementation is trivial because the idea is trivial: loop over entries and combine. What matters is what these operations mean geometrically.

The magnitude is the Euclidean length of the vector the distance from the origin to the point. The dot product of two vectors is the product of their magnitudes times the cosine of the angle between them. When the angle is zero the vectors point in exactly the same direction the cosine is 1 and the dot product is the product of the magnitudes. When the angle is 90 degrees, the cosine is 0 and the dot product is 0. When the angle is 180 degrees, the cosine is -1. The dot product is a measure of alignment. Same direction: positive. Perpendicular: zero. Opposite direction: negative.

Cosine similarity normalizes the magnitudes away. It tells you whether two vectors point in the same direction regardless of how long they are. This is the metric that drives every vector search system. When you query a vector database for the five most similar documents, the database is computing cosine similarities against thousands of vectors and returning the ones with the highest scores. If two things are close in embedding space high cosine similarity the model believes they are semantically similar. This is also how attention works in transformers: the model scores every pair of tokens with a dot product, then weights their values by those scores. We will build attention from scratch in Chapter 13. For now, internalize that the dot product is the engine of similarity.

A Matrix Is a Transformation

A vector sits still. A matrix moves it.

A matrix is a rectangle of numbers. An m x n matrix has m rows and n columns. When you multiply a matrix by a vector, you apply a transformation: rotation, scaling, shearing, projection, or some combination of all four. The output is a new vector, possibly in a different number of dimensions.

The shape rule for matrix-vector multiplication: an (m x n) matrix operates on an n-dimensional vector and produces an m-dimensional vector. The inner dimensions the n must match. Each entry in the output is the dot product of one row of the matrix with the input vector.

A 2x3 matrix transforms a 3D vector into a 2D vector:

[w00w01w02w10w11w12][x0x1x2]=[w00x0+w01x1+w02x2w10x0+w11x1+w12x2]\begin{bmatrix} w_{00} & w_{01} & w_{02} \\ w_{10} & w_{11} & w_{12} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_0 \\ x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{00}x_0 + w_{01}x_1 + w_{02}x_2 \\ w_{10}x_0 + w_{11}x_1 + w_{12}x_2 \end{bmatrix} [​w​00​​​w​10​​​​​w​01​​​w​11​​​​​w​02​​​w​12​​​​]​⎣​⎡​​​x​0​​​x​1​​​x​2​​​​​⎦​⎤​​=[​w​00​​x​0​​+w​01​​x​1​​+w​02​​x​2​​​w​10​​x​0​​+w​11​​x​1​​+w​12​​x​2​​​​]

This says: take each row of the weight matrix, compute its dot product with the input, and place the result in the output.

Now build it.

class Matrix:
    def __init__(self, rows):
        self.rows = [list(r) for r in rows]
        self.shape = (len(self.rows), len(self.rows[0]))

    def matvec(self, vector):
        return Vector([sum(r[j] * vector.components[j]
            for j in range(self.shape[1]))
            for r in self.rows])

    def matmul(self, other):
        return Matrix([
            [sum(self.rows[i][k] * other.rows[k][j]
                for k in range(self.shape[1]))
             for j in range(other.shape[1])]
            for i in range(self.shape[0])
        ])

    def transpose(self):
        return Matrix([
            [self.rows[j][i] for j in range(self.shape[0])]
            for i in range(self.shape[1])
        ])

    def __repr__(self):
        rows = "\n  ".join(str(r) for r in self.rows)
        return f"Matrix({self.shape}):\n  {rows}"

The matvec method is what a dense neural network layer does. If your layer has 128 inputs and 64 outputs, the weight matrix is (64, 128). The bias is a separate vector of length 64 added after the multiplication. The combined operation is output = weights.matvec(input) + bias. That is the entire forward pass of a linear layer, minus the nonlinearity.

Matrix-matrix multiplication is the composition of transformations. When you multiply matrix B by matrix A computing B @ A you get a single matrix that applies A first, then B. Composition is how deep models chain transformations. A 50-layer ResNet is 50 matrix multiplications chained together, with ReLUs in between. The chain makes sense because matrix multiplication is associative: (C @ B) @ A equals C @ (B @ A). It is not commutative: B @ A is generally not equal to A @ B. Order matters in neural networks the same way order matters in function composition. sin(cos(x)) is not cos(sin(x)).

The Determinant: When a Matrix Is Reversible

The transpose flips rows and columns. An m x n matrix becomes n x m. In backpropagation, the gradient through a layer is multiplied by the transpose of the weight matrix, not the matrix itself. The transpose is the reverse transformation not the inverse, but the adjoint under the dot product.

The determinant is a single number computed from a square matrix. It tells you one thing: whether the matrix is invertible. If the determinant is zero, the matrix is singular applying it destroys information, and there is no operation that can recover it. If the determinant is nonzero, an inverse exists.

For a 2x2 matrix:

det[abcd]=ad−bc\det \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = ad - bc det[​a​c​​​b​d​​]=ad−bc

If ad - bc is zero, the two rows are linearly dependent one row is a scalar multiple of the other. The transformation collapses the plane into a line, and you cannot recover the original point from a point on a line.

In practice: a matrix with determinant near zero is ill-conditioned. Small changes in the input cause large changes in the output. This is the linear algebra explanation for why models with correlated features produce unstable predictions. If two columns of your design matrix are nearly identical height in inches and height in centimeters the determinant of the covariance matrix is near zero, the linear system has no unique solution, and the learned weights can be huge (the model “compensates” by driving one weight up and the other down, which cancel on the training data but diverge on new data). Regularization L1, L2, ridge fixes this by adding a term that penalizes large weights, which numerically increases the effective determinant and stabilizes the solution.

def determinant_2x2(matrix):
    return matrix.rows[0][0] * matrix.rows[1][1] - matrix.rows[0][1] * matrix.rows[1][0]

def inverse_2x2(matrix):
    det = determinant_2x2(matrix)
    if det == 0:
        raise ValueError("Singular matrix   no inverse")
    return Matrix([
        [ matrix.rows[1][1] / det, -matrix.rows[0][1] / det],
        [-matrix.rows[1][0] / det,  matrix.rows[0][0] / det],
    ])

A = Matrix([[4, 7], [2, 6]])
print(f"A = {A.rows}")
print(f"det(A) = {determinant_2x2(A)}")
inv = inverse_2x2(A)
print(f"A @ A^-1 = {A.matmul(inv).rows}")

Rank: How Many Dimensions Your Matrix Actually Spans

A set of vectors is linearly independent if no vector can be written as a combination of the others. The rank of a matrix is the number of linearly independent columns equivalently, the number of linearly independent rows. If an (m x n) matrix has rank r < min(m, n), it is rank-deficient. Some columns carry no new information. The transformation discards dimensions.

In ML, the rank tells you whether your features are redundant and whether your linear system has a unique solution. If your feature matrix has 100 columns but rank 80, then 20 of your features are linear combinations of the other 80. They add noise without adding signal. The model cannot distinguish their individual effects any set of weights that produces the same linear combination produces the same output. This is multicollinearity.

Rank also explains the most influential trick in modern fine-tuning: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation). A weight matrix in a large language model might be 4096 x 4096 roughly 16 million parameters. LoRA replaces the full weight update with two smaller matrices: W = W_0 + A @ B, where A is (4096, r) and B is (r, 4096) with a small rank r, typically 8 or 16. The update lives in a low-dimensional subspace. Instead of learning all 16 million numbers, you learn 4096 x r + r x 4096 roughly 131,000 parameters for r=16. The intuition that makes this work: the weight update needed to adapt a pretrained model to a new task does not require the full rank of the weight matrix. It requires a small adjustment in a few key directions. You are exploiting the fact that the pretrained model has already learned the structure; fine-tuning is a low-rank perturbation. This is linear algebra doing real work at the scale of a GPT.

Gram-Schmidt and QR: Finding a Clean Basis

Given any set of linearly independent vectors, the Gram-Schmidt process converts them into an orthonormal basis a set of mutually perpendicular vectors, each of length 1. Orthonormal bases are numerically stable. When you solve linear systems in an orthonormal basis, rounding errors do not compound. When you project data onto an orthonormal basis (as PCA does), the components are independent and interpretable.

The algorithm: take the first vector. Normalize it divide by its magnitude so it has length 1. Take the second vector. Subtract its projection onto the first remove the component that overlaps with what you already have. Normalize the remainder. Take the third vector. Subtract its projections onto the first and second. Normalize. Repeat.

u1=v1∣v1∣u_1 = \frac{v_1}{|v_1|} u​1​​=​∣v​1​​∣​​v​1​​​​

u2=v2−(v2⋅u1)u1∣v2−(v2⋅u1)u1∣u_2 = \frac{v_2 - (v_2 \cdot u_1) u_1}{|v_2 - (v_2 \cdot u_1) u_1|} u​2​​=​∣v​2​​−(v​2​​⋅u​1​​)u​1​​∣​​v​2​​−(v​2​​⋅u​1​​)u​1​​​​

The numerator of u_2 removes everything from v_2 that is already explainable by u_1. The denominator re-normalizes.

def project(a, b):
    scalar = a.dot(b) / b.dot(b)
    return Vector([scalar * x for x in b.components])

def gram_schmidt(vectors):
    basis = []
    for v in vectors:
        w = v
        for u in basis:
            w = w - project(w, u)
        if w.magnitude() > 1e-10:
            basis.append(w.normalize())
    return basis

v1 = Vector([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])
v2 = Vector([1.0, 1.0, 0.0])
v3 = Vector([1.0, 1.0, 1.0])

basis = gram_schmidt([v1, v2, v3])
for i, u in enumerate(basis):
    print(f"u{i+1} = {u}, |u| = {u.magnitude():.6f}")
print(f"u0·u1 = {basis[0].dot(basis[1]):.6f}")

Gram-Schmidt is the conceptual algorithm. In production, you use QR decomposition the numerical version of the same idea. A matrix A is decomposed as A = Q @ R, where Q is an orthonormal matrix (the Gram-Schmidt output) and R is upper triangular (the projection coefficients). QR is used for solving least-squares problems, computing eigenvalues, and building stable numerical solvers. Every time scikit-learn fits a linear regression under the hood, it does not solve the normal equations directly with a matrix inverse. It calls a QR-based solver that is less sensitive to ill-conditioned matrices.

Transformations: Rotation, Scaling, Shear, Reflection

Every linear transformation is a matrix. Every matrix tells you where the standard basis vectors go after the transformation. The first column of a 2x2 matrix is the image of [1, 0]. The second column is the image of [0, 1]. Everything else follows.

A rotation matrix moves points along circles without changing distances:

R(θ)=[cosθ−sinθsinθcosθ]R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} R(θ)=[​cosθ​sinθ​​​−sinθ​cosθ​​]

For θ=90∘\theta = 90^\circθ=90​∘​​, cos=0\cos = 0cos=0, sin=1\sin = 1sin=1. The point [3, 1] becomes [-1, 3]. The distance from the origin stays the same. The determinant is always 1.

A scaling matrix stretches each axis independently:

S(sx,sy)=[sx00sy]S(s_x, s_y) = \begin{bmatrix} s_x & 0 \\ 0 & s_y \end{bmatrix} S(s​x​​,s​y​​)=[​s​x​​​0​​​0​s​y​​​​]

The determinant is s_x * s_y the factor by which area changes.

A shear matrix tilts one axis while keeping the other fixed. In the x-direction:

Hx(k)=[1k01]H_x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & k \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} H​x​​(k)=[​1​0​​​k​1​​]

The y-coordinate of every point is unchanged. The x-coordinate shifts by k * y. Rectangles become parallelograms. The determinant is 1 area is preserved.

A reflection matrix flips space across an axis:

My=[−1001]M_y = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} M​y​​=[​−1​0​​​0​1​​]

The determinant is -1 orientation is reversed.

Composition combines transformations. If you want to rotate a point 30 degrees and then scale it by a factor of 2 in x and 0.5 in y, you multiply the matrices: composed = S @ R. Apply it to a point: result = S @ (R @ point). The order is right-to-left in the multiplication: the point is rotated first, then scaled. Composition is why you can pack an entire neural network into a single computational graph each layer is a matrix, and the whole thing is a composition of matrices interrupted by element-wise nonlinearities.

import math

def rotation_2d(theta):
    c, s = math.cos(theta), math.sin(theta)
    return Matrix([[c, -s], [s, c]])

def scaling_2d(sx, sy):
    return Matrix([[sx, 0], [0, sy]])

point = Vector([2.0, 1.0])
R = rotation_2d(math.pi / 4)
S = scaling_2d(2, 0.5)

rotated = R.matvec(point)
print(f"R @ point = {rotated}")

composed = S.matmul(R)
result = composed.matvec(point)
print(f"S @ R @ point = {result}")

print(f"R @ S != S @ R: {R.matmul(S).matvec(point)} != {result}")

The last line prints two different results. Matrix multiplication is not commutative. Rotate-then-scale produces different coordinates than scale-then-rotate. This is why the order of operations in a neural network forward pass matters and why the order of gradients in backpropagation matters identically in reverse.

What a Tensor Actually Is

Vectors are rank 1. Matrices are rank 2. A tensor generalizes to any number of dimensions. A scalar is rank 0. A batch of 32 color images at 224x224 is rank 4: (32, 3, 224, 224). Each number is a dimension: batch index, color channel, row, column.

The shape is a tuple listing the size of each axis. The product of all sizes is the total number of elements. Strides tell the indexing logic how to jump through memory. For a row-major layout (PyTorch and NumPy’s default), the last axis has stride 1, the second-to-last has stride equal to the last axis size, and so on. A tensor of shape (3, 4) has strides (4, 1): to advance one row, skip 4 elements in the flat buffer. To advance one column, skip 1.

The practical implication: a transpose does not move data. It swaps the strides. A tensor that started as (3, 4) with strides (4, 1) becomes (4, 3) with strides (1, 4). The flat data stays in place. This makes transpose essentially free. It also means that some operations produce “non-contiguous” tensors where elements of a single row are not adjacent in memory, which matters when you hand the tensor to a C-level operation like BLAS. This is why PyTorch sometimes gives you RuntimeError: expected a contiguous tensor. The fix is .contiguous(), which copies the data into a contiguous buffer not free, but explicit about the cost.

Reshape changes the shape without changing the total number of elements. It is the operation that makes batch processing work: a list of B vectors of length D can be stacked into a (B, D) matrix for a single batched matrix multiply, which is far faster than looping over individual vectors.

Broadcasting: The Rule That Stops Shape Errors

Broadcasting is the mechanism that lets you add a bias vector of length 64 to a matrix of shape (32, 64) a batch of 32 vectors, each being transformed by a shared bias. The shapes do not match: (32, 64) plus (64,). Broadcasting aligns the shapes from the right, pads the smaller shape with 1s on the left, and stretches dimensions of size 1 to match the other array.

A shape:   (32, 64)
B shape:        (64)
Padded B:  ( 1, 64)
Broadcast: (32, 64)

The bias vector is conceptually copied 32 times once per row. No memory is actually copied. The iteration logic just repeats the same data.

The full rule: two dimensions are compatible if they are equal, or if one of them is 1. Broadcasting produces the element-wise maximum along each axis. This is the source of most cryptic shape errors. You intended to broadcast along the second axis but actually broadcast along the first. The operation does not error it produces the wrong answer, silently. The defense is to verify shapes mentally before you run the code, and to use .shape liberally during development.

Einsum: One Function, Every Tensor Operation

Einstein summation notation labels each axis of each tensor with a letter. Axes appearing in the inputs but not the output are summed over. Axes appearing in both are preserved. The notation compresses dozens of tensor operations into a single string.

"i,i->"       dot product
"i,j->ij"     outer product
"ij,jk->ik"   matrix multiply
"bij,bjk->bik" batch matrix multiply
"bhtd,bhsd->bhts" attention scores
"bij->bji"    transpose each matrix in a batch
"ii->"        trace

The string "bij,bjk->bik" says: there are two tensors, A and B. The first axes of both are batch dimensions labeled b they must have the same size. A’s second axis is i, third is j. B’s second is j, third is k. Sum over j (it appears in the inputs but not in the output). Keep b, i, and k. The result has shape (b, i, k).

This is a batch matrix multiply. If you have 32 training examples and each one needs a matrix multiplication a linear layer processing a batch you could loop over the batch dimension. Or you could write one einsum call that does all 32 in parallel. The einsum version is typically faster because the underlying library can fuse operations and avoid intermediate allocations.

In PyTorch:

import torch

b, i, j, k = 32, 128, 64, 256
A = torch.randn(b, i, j)
B = torch.randn(b, j, k)
C = torch.einsum("bij,bjk->bik", A, B)
print(C.shape)

The output shape is (32, 128, 256). The j axis 64 was summed away.

Einsum is worth learning because it replaces a dozen NumPy/PyTorch functions (dot, matmul, bmm, outer, trace, transpose) with one uniform syntax. It also makes the intent visibly clear in code review: "bhtd,bhsd->bhts" announces “this is computing attention scores between queries and keys” in a way that a sequence of reshapes and permutes does not.

Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors: What the Matrix Reveals About Itself

Most vectors change direction when a matrix hits them. Eigenvectors are the exceptions: they emerge from the transformation pointing in the same direction, only scaled. The scaling factor is the eigenvalue.

A⋅v=λ⋅vA \cdot v = \lambda \cdot v A⋅v=λ⋅v

For a 2x2 matrix, the eigenvalues are the roots of the characteristic equation:

λ2−tr(A)λ+det(A)=0\lambda^2 - \text{tr}(A)\lambda + \det(A) = 0 λ​2​​−tr(A)λ+det(A)=0

where tr(A) is the trace the sum of the diagonal entries.

def eigenvalues_2x2(matrix):
    a, b = matrix.rows[0]
    c, d = matrix.rows[1]
    trace = a + d
    det = a * d - b * c
    disc = trace**2 - 4 * det
    if disc < 0:
        real = trace / 2
        imag = ((-disc)**0.5) / 2
        return (complex(real, imag), complex(real, -imag))
    sqrt_disc = disc**0.5
    return ((trace + sqrt_disc) / 2, (trace - sqrt_disc) / 2)

A = Matrix([[2, 1], [1, 2]])
vals = eigenvalues_2x2(A)
print(f"Eigenvalues: {vals}")

For A = [[2, 1], [1, 2]], the eigenvalues are 3 and 1. The eigenvector for lambda=3 is [1, 1] along the diagonal, the matrix stretches by 3x. The eigenvector for lambda=1 is [1, -1] perpendicular to the diagonal, the matrix leaves it unchanged. Every other direction is a mix of these two fundamental directions, stretched by a mix of the two eigenvalues.

Why eigenvalues matter for AI has three answers.

First: PCA. The principal components of a dataset are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. The eigenvalue of each component is the variance explained. Sort by eigenvalue, keep the top k, and you have dimensionality reduction that preserves the most information. Chapter 5 covers this in detail.

Second: stability. In recurrent neural networks, the weight matrix is applied repeatedly at every time step. If any eigenvalue has magnitude greater than 1, the activations explode with time. If all eigenvalues have magnitude less than 1, the activations vanish. This is the vanishing/exploding gradient problem stated at the level of linear algebra. It generalizes to deep feedforward networks too: if the eigenvalues of each weight matrix are not near 1 on average, the gradients scale exponentially with depth, and training becomes impossible beyond a certain layer count. Residual connections and layer normalization are both designed to keep the effective eigenvalues near 1.

Third: spectral methods. Graph neural networks, spectral clustering, and signal processing on graphs all decompose the graph Laplacian a matrix derived from the graph’s adjacency information into eigenvalues and eigenvectors. The eigenvalues encode the connectivity structure. The eigenvectors encode the community structure. We touch on this in Chapter 5 and again in Volume 2 when we do graph-based retrieval for RAG.

Eigendecomposition: Separating Rotation from Scaling

If a matrix has a full set of eigenvectors, it can be decomposed into three pieces:

A=V⋅D⋅V−1A = V \cdot D \cdot V^{-1} A=V⋅D⋅V​−1​​

V is the matrix whose columns are the eigenvectors. D is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues. The decomposition says: applying A is equivalent to rotating into the eigenvector coordinate system, scaling along each eigenvector by its eigenvalue, and rotating back. The eigenvectors tell you the directions of pure scaling. The eigenvalues tell you how much scaling in each direction.

This is why the eigendecomposition is the heart of PCA. The covariance matrix is always symmetric, so its eigenvectors are orthonormal V is a rotation matrix. D tells you how much variance lives in each rotated direction. Truncate to the top k eigenvalues, and you have the best k-dimensional approximation of the data.

From Scratch to Library: NumPy and PyTorch

Everything you built from scratch in this chapter is implemented, optimized, and hardened in NumPy and PyTorch. You wrote a Matrix class to understand the semantics. Now throw it away and use the library. The library versions are 100x faster (written in C and Fortran via BLAS), more numerically stable (using decades of numerical linear algebra research), and integrate with automatic differentiation and GPU acceleration.

import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 2, 3], dtype=float)
