Depth estimation feeds into AR/VR (place virtual objects with correct occlusion), robotics (collision avoidance), 3D reconstruction (lift a 2D image into a 3D scene), and any vision-language model that wants to reason about spatial structure.

Self-supervised vision: pretraining without labels

Most of vision’s foundation models in 2026 are pretrained without human labels. Three families dominate:

Masked image modeling (MAE, BEiT, SimMIM): mask 60-75% of image patches, train the encoder to reconstruct the missing patches from the visible ones. Conceptually identical to BERT’s masked language modeling. Yields strong fine-tuning representations.

Contrastive learning (SimCLR, MoCo, BYOL): train an encoder so two augmentations of the same image have similar embeddings, augmentations of different images have dissimilar embeddings. The basis of CLIP’s image side and the workhorse for general-purpose features.

DINO and self-distillation (Caron et al., 2021; DINOv2 and DINOv3 follow): a teacher-student architecture where the teacher is an EMA of the student, both look at different crops of the same image, and the student learns to predict the teacher’s embedding. Produces remarkably strong features without contrastive negatives.

In 2026, DINOv3 and MAE-V2 are the boring defaults for “I want a strong general image encoder I can fine-tune on my domain.” For multimodal work, CLIP and SigLIP remain the boring defaults because their text-aligned embeddings are exactly what downstream multimodal tasks want.

Image generation: GANs, briefly

A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) is two networks in a game. The generator maps noise to images. The discriminator judges whether an image came from the generator or from real data. Train both alternately: the generator improves at fooling the discriminator, the discriminator improves at catching the generator. At equilibrium, the generator produces samples indistinguishable from real data.

GANs ruled image generation from 2014 to 2020. DCGAN, StyleGAN (and v2/v3), BigGAN are the canonical names. They produce sharp, fast samples a single forward pass of the generator, no iteration and are notoriously hard to train. Mode collapse, training instability, hyperparameter sensitivity, missing diversity: GAN training is a craft you learn over years.

By 2026 GANs are mostly historical for unconditional generation. They survive in two specialized roles. StyleGAN3 is still the boring default for face manipulation and style transfer where its disentangled latent space matters. Adversarial diffusion distillation (SD-Turbo, FLUX-schnell) uses a discriminator to distill many-step diffusion models into 1-4 step ones; the GAN component lives on inside diffusion’s serving stack.

The reason diffusion overtook GANs is that diffusion has a single MSE loss, no adversarial dynamics, and trains stably on any data with any architecture. Mode collapse is essentially impossible. The cost is iterative sampling but DDIM, then rectified flow, then distillation closed that gap. Diffusion won.

Diffusion models: the dominant generative paradigm

A diffusion model learns to denoise. Take a clean image, add noise repeatedly until you have pure Gaussian noise, then train a network to reverse the process one tiny step at a time. Sample by starting from noise and denoising for TTT steps. That’s the whole idea.

Forward process

A clean image x0x_0x​0​​ becomes x1,x2,…,xTx_1, x_2, \ldots, x_Tx​1​​,x​2​​,…,x​T​​ by adding Gaussian noise:

q(xt∣xt−1)=N(xt;1−βtxt−1,βtI)q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x_t; \sqrt{1 - \beta_t}\, x_{t-1},\, \beta_t I\right) q(x​t​​∣x​t−1​​)=N(x​t​​;√​1−β​t​​​​​x​t−1​​,β​t​​I)

with a small variance schedule βt\beta_tβ​t​​, typically linear from 10−410^{-4}10​−4​​ to 0.020.020.02 over T=1000T = 1000T=1000 steps. The clean image is gradually corrupted; by step TTT it is indistinguishable from pure Gaussian noise.

A useful identity, which makes training tractable: you can sample xtx_tx​t​​ directly from x0x_0x​0​​ in one step. Define αt=1−βt\alpha_t = 1 - \beta_tα​t​​=1−β​t​​, α¯t=∏s=1tαs\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^t \alpha_s​α​¯​​​t​​=∏​s=1​t​​α​s​​. Then:

xt=α¯tx0+1−α¯tϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I).x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\, x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}\, \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I). x​t​​=√​​α​¯​​​t​​​​​x​0​​+√​1−​α​¯​​​t​​​​​ϵ,ϵ∼N(0,I).

This single equation is what makes diffusion practical. During training you pick a random ttt, sample xtx_tx​t​​ in one step, and predict the noise ϵ\epsilonϵ. No simulation of the full Markov chain.

Training and sampling

The loss is shockingly simple:

L=Et,x0,ϵ[∥ϵ−ϵθ(xt,t)∥2]\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \epsilon}\!\left[\|\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\|^2\right] L=E​t,x​0​​,ϵ​​[∥ϵ−ϵ​θ​​(x​t​​,t)∥​2​​]

Predict the noise. MSE. Done. There is no adversarial game, no collapse, no oscillation. A small team can train a diffusion model and get reasonable samples. Training ImageNet-class diffusion models on a single GPU is now a coursework exercise.

Sampling walks back from xT∼N(0,I)x_T \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)x​T​​∼N(0,I) one step at a time, using the predicted noise to denoise. The DDPM update is:

xt−1=1αt(xt−βt1−α¯tϵθ(xt,t))+βtz,z∼N(0,I)x_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}\!\left(x_t - \frac{\beta_t}{\sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}} \epsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\right) + \sqrt{\beta_t}\, z, \quad z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) x​t−1​​=​√​α​t​​​​​​​1​​(x​t​​−​√​1−​α​¯​​​t​​​​​​​β​t​​​​ϵ​θ​​(x​t​​,t))+√​β​t​​​​​z,z∼N(0,I)

The ugly coefficients fall out of Bayes’ rule on the Gaussian forward chain. Run this for T=1000T = 1000T=1000 steps and you get an image. Slow.

Faster samplers: DDIM and beyond

DDIM (Song et al., 2020) defined a deterministic reverse process that skips timesteps without retraining. 50 DDIM steps reach near-1000-step DDPM quality. DPM-Solver, DPM-Solver++, and Euler-ancestral samplers further reduce step counts to 20-30 with high quality. Production systems never use the original DDPM sampler.

Classifier-free guidance

To make a model follow a prompt strongly, train it with the conditioning randomly dropped (e.g., 10% of the time the model sees no prompt). At sampling time, run the model twice once conditional, once unconditional and extrapolate:

ϵguided=ϵuncond+w⋅(ϵcond−ϵuncond)\epsilon_{\text{guided}} = \epsilon_{\text{uncond}} + w \cdot (\epsilon_{\text{cond}} - \epsilon_{\text{uncond}}) ϵ​guided​​=ϵ​uncond​​+w⋅(ϵ​cond​​−ϵ​uncond​​)

The guidance scale www controls how strongly the model follows the prompt. w=1w = 1w=1 means just conditional; w=7.5w = 7.5w=7.5 was the SD 1.5 default; w=3.5w = 3.5w=3.5 is the modern default for rectified-flow models. Higher www means more prompt adherence at the cost of diversity and (eventually) artifacts.

This is the single most important inference-time knob in any text-to-image system. Half the production prompt engineering for image generation is finding the right guidance scale.

Stable Diffusion: latent diffusion in production

Pixel-space diffusion is expensive: a 512×512 RGB image is a 786,432-dimensional tensor, and the U-Net has to operate at that scale for TTT steps. Latent diffusion (Rombach et al., 2022, the paper behind Stable Diffusion) does the diffusion in the latent space of an autoencoder, not in pixel space.

The architecture has three parts:

  1. A VAE (variational autoencoder) compresses 512×512×3 images into 64×64×4 latents (a ∼48×\sim 48 \times∼48× spatial compression). The VAE is trained once and frozen.
  2. A denoising U-Net runs the diffusion process in the 64×64×4 latent space. This is what’s actually trained.
  3. A text encoder (CLIP for SD 1.5; T5+CLIP for SD3 and FLUX) provides text conditioning, injected into the U-Net via cross-attention.
prompt -> text encoder -> conditioning
                                    \
noise (64x64x4) -> U-Net (1000 steps) -> latent -> VAE decoder -> image (512x512x3)

The 48× compression makes diffusion 48× cheaper. Inference on a consumer GPU goes from “minutes per image” to “seconds per image.” Fine-tuning becomes affordable. The Stable Diffusion ecosystem LoRAs, ControlNets, IP-Adapter, custom checkpoints, the entire prompt-engineering subculture is built on this single architectural choice.

The library to know is diffusers (Hugging Face):

from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline
import torch

pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(
    "stabilityai/stable-diffusion-3-medium-diffusers",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
).to("cuda")

image = pipe(
    prompt="a photograph of a sunset over mountains, cinematic lighting, 4k",
    num_inference_steps=28,
    guidance_scale=4.5,
).images[0]

diffusers ships every major image and video diffusion model under one API. The boring default for “I want to generate images programmatically” in 2026.

Diffusion Transformers and Rectified Flow: the 2026 stack

Every state-of-the-art text-to-image model in 2026 has moved past U-Net DDPM. Two changes account for most of the move.

From U-Net to DiT

DiT (Diffusion Transformer, Peebles & Xie 2023) replaces the U-Net with a ViT-like transformer on the latent patches. Attention over the entire latent at every layer, conditioning injected via adaptive LayerNorm (predict scale and shift from the timestep and class embedding, apply them after LayerNorm).

class AdaLN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, cond_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(dim, elementwise_affine=False)
        self.modulator = nn.Linear(cond_dim, dim * 2)

    def forward(self, x, cond):
        scale, shift = self.modulator(cond).chunk(2, dim=-1)
        return self.norm(x) * (1 + scale.unsqueeze(1)) + shift.unsqueeze(1)

DiTs scale better than U-Nets. They handle text conditioning more cleanly through joint or cross attention. They produce SD3, FLUX, SD4, Z-Image, Qwen-Image, and Hunyuan-Image. The U-Net era of image generation effectively ended in 2024.

MMDiT (SD3) uses two parallel streams with separate weights for text and image tokens that share a joint attention. FLUX uses double-stream blocks early and single-stream (concatenated, shared-weight) blocks later for efficiency at depth.

Rectified Flow: the noise-to-data straight line

DDPM’s forward process is a curved trajectory in the space of distributions. The reverse process inherits the same curvature, which is why so many sampling steps are needed.

Rectified Flow (Liu et al., 2022; Esser et al. 2024 for SD3) defines a straight-line interpolation between data and noise:

xt=(1−t)x0+tϵ,t∈[0,1].x_t = (1 - t)\, x_0 + t\, \epsilon, \quad t \in [0, 1]. x​t​​=(1−t)x​0​​+tϵ,t∈[0,1].

The model predicts the velocity vθ(xt,t)=ϵ−x0v_\theta(x_t, t) = \epsilon - x_0v​θ​​(x​t​​,t)=ϵ−x​0​​. Sampling integrates this velocity backward and because the trajectory is essentially straight, far fewer integration steps are needed. SD3 and FLUX sample in 20-30 Euler steps where SD 1.5 needed 50 DDIM steps for similar quality.

Distillation: 1-4 step models

Latent Consistency Models (LCM), SDXL Turbo, FLUX Schnell: all train a student model that takes 1-4 steps to produce what the teacher takes 20-50 steps to produce. The training uses adversarial diffusion distillation (a discriminator on the student’s outputs) or consistency distillation (the student must produce the same final image regardless of starting noise level). FLUX Schnell, in 2026, is the boring default for real-time image generation.

Conditioning beyond text: ControlNet, IP-Adapter, LoRA

Text prompts are a low-bandwidth way to specify what you want in an image. Three add-ons turn diffusion into a controllable system.

ControlNet (Zhang et al., 2023) takes a structural conditioning input a Canny edge map, a depth map, an OpenPose skeleton, a segmentation mask, a sketch and threads it into every layer of a frozen diffusion U-Net or DiT through a parallel “control branch.” Sample with the same text prompt and the structural condition together, and the output respects both. ControlNet is what made diffusion useful for design work; you can lock the composition with a depth map and let the model fill in the textures and lighting.

IP-Adapter (Ye et al., 2023) does the same for image conditioning. Feed it a reference photo (a face, an art style, a product); it produces an embedding that influences generation toward that reference. Combined with text, it gives you “this composition (text), in this style (image)” in one pipeline. The boring default for style-transfer-meets-text-to-image.

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation, Hu et al., 2021): the lightweight fine-tuning method that let the diffusion-art subculture fine-tune SD 1.5 and SDXL on consumer GPUs. Instead of updating the full U-Net, LoRA learns small low-rank matrices added to the attention weights typically 5-50 MB per LoRA versus 5+ GB per full model. You can train a LoRA on 20 images of a specific person, place, or art style in 30 minutes on a 4090. We come back to LoRA at length in Volume 2 when fine-tuning LLMs; the math is identical.

Inpainting and img2img are pipeline variants, not architectures. Inpainting masks part of an existing image and asks the model to fill the masked region (using SAM-derived masks plus an inpainting-trained checkpoint). img2img starts the diffusion process from a partially-noised version of an existing image, preserving its global structure while adding new detail. Both are inference-time modifications to the standard sampling loop, no retraining required.

The combination base model + LoRA + ControlNet + IP-Adapter + inpainting is what every production diffusion workflow looks like in 2026. ComfyUI and Automatic1111 are the open-source GUIs that compose these.

The 2026 model landscape

Model Size Architecture Notes
Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large 8B MMDiT SAI’s flagship
FLUX.1 dev / pro 12B Double + single-stream DiT Frontier quality, BFL
FLUX.1 schnell 12B Distilled 1-4 step Apache 2.0, real-time
Z-Image 6B Single-stream DiT Efficiency frontier
Qwen-Image 20B DiT + Qwen text tower Open, strong text rendering
Hunyuan-Image-3.0 ~80B DiT Tencent, research

For most 2026 work, FLUX.1-dev for quality, FLUX.1-schnell for speed, SD3.5 when permissive licensing matters most.

Video understanding and video diffusion

Video adds a temporal dimension to everything in this chapter so far. The architectures factor into two families.

Video understanding (classification, action recognition, retrieval): the standard architectures are VideoMAE (masked autoencoding extended to video patches) and VJEPA (joint embedding predictive architecture, Meta). They learn from massive unlabeled video and produce features that fine-tune well for downstream tasks. TimeSformer and MViT are the convolutional / hybrid baselines.

Video generation: the explosive 2024-2026 frontier. Sora (OpenAI, 2024) showed that diffusion transformers, scaled up and trained on enough video, produce minute-long coherent video clips from text prompts. Veo (Google), Kling (Kuaishou), Sora 2 (OpenAI 2025), Hunyuan Video, Wan 2 (Alibaba), and Open-Sora (open-source) are the 2026 leaders. The architectures are space-time transformers: each video frame becomes a grid of spatiotemporal patches, attention runs across all patches in all frames, conditioning includes both text and (for some models) keyframes or camera trajectories.

A subset of these models Genie 2 (DeepMind), GameNGen, and various open research projects push toward world models: video diffusion that responds to user actions. Give the model a frame and an action (“turn left, walk forward”), and it generates the next frame as if you were inside an interactive simulation. World models are the bridge between video generation and embodied AI; they show up in robotics planning, game AI, and the long-running dream of “predict-the-future” world simulators.

For most CV engineers in 2026, video generation is use-the-API territory rather than train-your-own. The compute requirements are extreme; the open-source ecosystem is real but heavy. We come back to video in Volume 2 when we discuss multimodal AI.

3D vision: NeRF and Gaussian Splatting

3D reconstruction takes a set of 2D images of a scene and produces a representation you can render from new viewpoints. The two breakthrough representations of the last five years.

NeRF (Neural Radiance Fields, Mildenhall et al. 2020) represents a scene as a small MLP that, given a 3D position (x,y,z)(x, y, z)(x,y,z) and a view direction (θ,ϕ)(\theta, \phi)(θ,ϕ), outputs a color and a density. To render an image, you cast a ray for each pixel, sample 3D points along it, query the MLP at each, and volume-render the colors and densities into a final pixel value.

NeRF works astonishingly well for photorealistic novel-view synthesis from a few dozen photos. The original was slow to train (hours per scene) and slow to render (seconds per frame); Instant NGP (Müller et al. 2022) made it minutes-per-scene and real-time. NeRF unlocked an entire research subfield view synthesis, 3D reconstruction, scene editing and is the boring default for “I have photos and I want a 3D scene I can navigate.”

3D Gaussian Splatting (Kerbl et al. 2023) replaces the MLP with an explicit set of millions of 3D Gaussians, each with a position, covariance, color, and opacity. Rendering composites Gaussians via a fast tile-based rasterizer. Training is faster than NeRF; rendering is real-time at 100+ FPS; quality is competitive. By 2026, 3D Gaussian Splatting is the production default for 3D capture and novel-view synthesis; NeRF survives in research and in scenarios where the implicit representation matters (e.g., joint geometry-and-appearance editing).

For CV engineers, the libraries to know are nerfstudio (NeRF + many variants under one API) and gsplat (3D Gaussian Splatting). Both run on a single GPU; capture-to-renderable-scene takes minutes for a small object, hours for a large scene.

Vision-Language Models: ViT-MLP-LLM

The single most consequential vision development of 2024-2026 is the Vision-Language Model: an image encoder bolted to a full language model, trained to answer arbitrary questions about images. CLIP gave you a shared embedding space; a VLM gives you a generative model that can describe, reason about, and act on visual input.

The architecture is the same across the open-source frontier: ViT-MLP-LLM.

image -> ViT (CLIP-L, SigLIP, DINOv3) -> patch tokens (N, d_vit)
                                              |
                                              v
                                          MLP projector (2-4 layers)
                                              |
                                              v
                                      tokens in LLM space (N, d_llm)
                                              |
                                              v
text prompt -> tokenizer -> text tokens (M, d_llm)
                                              |
                                              v
                              concat / interleave -> decoder LLM -> generated text

The vision encoder produces patch tokens. A small MLP projects them into the LLM’s embedding dimension. The language model treats projected vision tokens as prefix context and generates an answer. Training is staged: align (freeze ViT and LLM, train projector), pretrain (unfreeze on interleaved image-text data), and instruction-tune (fine-tune on (image, question, answer) triples).

The 2026 model landscape:

Model Params Strength
Qwen3-VL-8B 8B Strong dense default, GUI-agent capable
Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B (MoE) 235B/22B Frontier open VLM
InternVL3.5 38-241B Top open MMBench scores
LLaVA-Next 72B 72B Easy to fine-tune, permissive
GLM-4.6V ~70B Strong OCR and document
MiniCPM-V-2.6 8B Edge-friendly
GPT-4V / GPT-5 Closed, frontier
Gemini 2.5 Pro Closed, frontier multimodal

Two ideas worth flagging. DeepStack (Qwen3-VL): instead of projecting only the last ViT layer, project tokens from multiple ViT depths and stack them. Deeper layers carry semantics; shallower layers carry spatial detail. Both matter. Visual agents: the same VLMs trained on screenshots-plus-actions can operate GUIs (click, type, scroll), which is the vision side of every “AI PC” demo you’ll see in 2026.

The VLM failure mode worth budgeting for is hallucination: the model confidently says things not grounded in the image. Skywork.ai introduced Cross-Modal Error Rate (CMER) the fraction of outputs where text confidence is high but image-text similarity (via a CLIP-family checker) is low and showed that routing high-CMER outputs to human review cuts production hallucinations by ~35%. If you’re shipping a VLM in production, you need a hallucination metric; CMER or a domain-specific equivalent.

VLMs are full citizens of the LLM stack. Volume 2 covers their internals, training, and deployment in detail. The thing to know now is the architecture.

A working VLM call, end to end, with transformers:

import torch
from transformers import AutoProcessor, AutoModelForVision2Seq
from PIL import Image

model_id = "Qwen/Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct"
processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForVision2Seq.from_pretrained(
    model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="auto"
)

image = Image.open("invoice.jpg")
messages = [{
    "role": "user",
    "content": [
        {"type": "image", "image": image},
        {"type": "text",  "text": "Extract the total amount, vendor name, and invoice date as JSON."},
    ],
}]
inputs = processor.apply_chat_template(
    messages, add_generation_prompt=True, tokenize=True, return_tensors="pt"
).to(model.device)
output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=512)
print(processor.decode(output[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

That is the entire VLM API surface from a developer’s perspective. Image goes in, text comes out. The model handles OCR, layout, reasoning, and structured output in one pass. This is why VLMs are eating so many specialized vision pipelines: a single API replaces a chain of OCR + layout-detection + field-extraction + format-translation models, often at higher quality.

The trade-off is cost and latency. A 70B-parameter VLM costs tens of cents per image and takes seconds per query; a specialized OCR pipeline costs fractions of a cent and runs in tens of milliseconds. The right answer is usually a cascade: cheap specialized model first, fall back to the VLM when the specialized model is uncertain or the task requires reasoning.

Real-time and edge deployment

Most CV is not deployed in the cloud on H100s. It runs on phones, dashcams, security cameras, edge boxes, and embedded hardware where every megabyte and millisecond matters. The toolkit for getting there:

Quantization. Convert weights from fp32/bf16 to int8 or int4. Speed: 2-4x. Memory: 4-8x. Quality cost: small to negligible for int8, sometimes meaningful for int4. Post-training quantization (calibrate on a small set, no retraining) is the boring default; quantization-aware training (simulate quantization during fine-tuning) recovers the last 1-2% of accuracy. PyTorch’s torch.ao.quantization, ONNX Runtime, and TensorRT all do this.

Pruning. Drop weights or entire channels that contribute little. Structured pruning (whole channels) gives real speedups; unstructured pruning gives compression but rarely runtime wins on standard hardware.

Knowledge distillation. Train a small “student” model to mimic a large “teacher” model’s outputs. The student often performs almost as well as the teacher at a fraction of the size. The recipe behind FLUX-schnell, MobileViT, and many production deployments.

Compilation. Convert PyTorch models to ONNX, then to TensorRT (NVIDIA), CoreML (Apple), TFLite (mobile), NNAPI (Android). Compilation does graph fusion, kernel selection, and hardware-specific optimization. Speedups of 2-10x are typical, with no quality loss.

Architecture choice. The best speedup is choosing a small model in the first place. MobileNetV3, EfficientNet-Lite, MobileViT, ConvNeXt-Tiny, YOLOv11n (“nano”) are designed for edge. They’re 5-50x smaller than their server-class siblings and 90-95% as accurate.

For most edge deployments the workflow is: pick a small architecture, fine-tune on your data, quantize to int8, export to ONNX, compile with the target runtime, profile end-to-end, iterate. The whole pipeline is a few days of engineering, not a research project. Volume 3 covers this in production-grade detail.

Evaluation across CV tasks

Each task family in this chapter has its own metrics, and choosing the wrong one is the most common reason a “good” model fails in production.

Task Primary metric Practical advice
Classification Top-1, Top-5, balanced accuracy Always look at per-class accuracy and confusion matrix
Detection mAP@0.5, mAP@[0.5:0.95] Report COCO-style mAP and per-class AP separately
Semantic seg mIoU, pixel accuracy mIoU dominates; pixel accuracy is misleading on class-imbalanced data
Instance seg mAP on masks Same machinery as detection, but IoU is on masks not boxes
Pose PCK@0.1, OKS OKS is the COCO standard; PCK is interpretable
OCR Character/word error rate (CER/WER) Compute on a held-out sample by hand at first
Retrieval Recall@K, NDCG@K Recall@K is the boring default; report multiple K
Tracking MOTA, IDF1, HOTA HOTA is the modern unified metric
Depth AbsRel, δ < 1.25, RMSE Domain-specific; check log-scale errors too
Generation FID, CLIP-Score, Aesthetic All flawed; pair with human evaluation
VLM MMMU, MMBench, DocVQA Use the benchmark closest to your domain

A few cross-cutting practical rules.

Always compute confidence intervals on metrics, especially for detection and segmentation where mAP fluctuates several points across runs. Bootstrap as in Chapter 5.

Always have a human-eval loop for generation. FID and CLIP-Score correlate with quality only weakly; the model-arena pattern (show two outputs side-by-side, ask which is better) is the gold standard.

Evaluate on data that looks like your production traffic, not on the academic benchmark. A YOLO that gets 0.55 mAP on COCO might get 0.30 on grainy convenience-store CCTV footage, and the second number is what you actually need to know.

Track metrics by subgroup: per-class, per-camera, per-time-of-day, per-skin-tone, per-language. Aggregate metrics hide failures on minority slices, and most production CV failures are on minority slices.

Data and labeling: the unsexy bottleneck

Most CV projects fail not at the model but at the data. Three things you should know about labeling and data engineering.

Pre-labeling with foundation models. The 2026 boring default is to run SAM, Grounding DINO, or a strong VLM on your unlabeled data first, then have humans correct the errors instead of labeling from scratch. This is 5-10x faster than blank-slate labeling and produces equally-good labels for most tasks. Tools like Label Studio, CVAT, and Roboflow Annotate all support pre-labeling out of the box.

Active learning. Train an initial model on a small labeled set; use it to predict on the unlabeled pool; pick the most-uncertain (or most-disagreed-on-by-an-ensemble) examples for the next round of labeling; repeat. Active learning typically reaches a target accuracy with 30-50% less labeled data than random sampling. The implementation is “uncertainty sampling” in scikit-learn or modAL; the harder part is engineering the iterative labeling loop.

Class imbalance and long tails. Real-world CV datasets are heavy-tailed: 90% of examples are 5 common classes, 1000 rare classes share the remaining 10%. The fixes from Chapter 6 (class weights, threshold tuning, focal loss) all apply, plus two CV-specific moves: class-balanced sampling (each batch contains a roughly equal number of each class) and strong augmentation on rare classes.

Labeling guidelines matter more than labeling tools. A team of three labelers with no written guideline will produce three inconsistent datasets. A written guideline with edge cases, examples, and a confidence-rating field will produce one. Spend the first week of any labeling project writing the guideline; iterate it as the team finds disagreements.

Synthetic data is the boring default for rare events. Use diffusion models, simulators (Unity, Isaac Sim), or composited backgrounds-plus-foregrounds to generate training data for cases the real world doesn’t supply often enough. Combined with a small set of real labels for fine-tuning, synthetic data is how you get production CV systems to handle the long tail.

We come back to data labeling and active learning at production scale in Volume 3. The thing to internalize now is that the data work is most of the project, and there is now a generation of foundation models that can do most of the labeling for you.

The vision pipeline as a system

Real CV systems are not single models. They are pipelines: detection feeds segmentation feeds OCR feeds retrieval feeds a VLM, with caches between each stage and a human-review queue at the end. Two patterns recur often enough to call out.

Cascaded detection. A fast detector finds candidate regions; a slower, more accurate model verifies them. Skip the slow model on regions the fast one is confident about. Standard for security cameras (motion detection → person classifier) and document processing (page detector → field extractor → OCR).

Embedding-first. Embed every image with a strong general encoder once; cache the embedding. Downstream tasks (retrieval, classification, clustering, deduplication) operate on embeddings, not on images. Massively cheaper at scale; the boring default for any pipeline with millions of images.

The system-design lessons matter as much as the model-design ones. A good vision system in 2026 is half models, half plumbing: caches, queues, monitoring, retraining schedules, dataset versioning, evaluation harnesses, drift detection. We come back to all of this in Volume 3.

What you’d actually use

A summary of the 2026 boring defaults, by job:

Job Default Notes
Real-time detection YOLOv11 (Ultralytics) Fast, mature, easy
Server-class detection RT-DETR or DINO NMS-free, top accuracy
Open-vocabulary detection Grounding DINO or YOLO-World Text-prompted
Semantic segmentation Mask2Former or SegFormer Mask2Former for accuracy, SegFormer for speed
Instance segmentation Mask2Former Unified model for all segmentation tasks
Interactive segmentation SAM 2 / SAM 3 Click, box, or text prompted
Pose estimation RTMPose or MediaPipe RTMPose for accuracy, MediaPipe for mobile
OCR PaddleOCR or GOT-OCR2.0 Paddle for production, GOT for end-to-end
Image retrieval DINOv2/CLIP + FAISS Embedding + ANN
Multi-object tracking YOLOv11 + ByteTrack Real-time, robust
Monocular depth Depth Anything V2 Foundation depth model
Image generation FLUX.1-dev or SD3.5 FLUX for quality, SD for licensing
Real-time generation FLUX.1-schnell or SD-Turbo Distilled 1-4 step
3D from photos Gaussian Splatting (gsplat) Real-time, training-fast
Vision-language Qwen3-VL or GPT-5 Open-source frontier vs. closed
Edge inference YOLOv11n + ONNX + TensorRT Standard pipeline

If you forget everything else: ViT or ConvNeXt for backbones, YOLO for real-time, Mask2Former for segmentation, SAM for masks, FLUX for images, Qwen-VL or its descendants for image-and-text, Depth Anything for depth, Gaussian Splatting for 3D, FAISS for retrieval, ONNX-plus-TensorRT for deployment. That single mental map covers most of production CV in 2026.

Sharp edges

A list of the failures this chapter exists to prevent.

Mismatched preprocessing. Loading a model pretrained with one normalization and serving it with another. Always check the model card.

NMS threshold defaults. YOLO, DETR, and friends ship with conservative IoU thresholds for benchmarks. Production needs tuning per domain. Check your false-positive and false-negative rates against your actual metric.

Mask resolution. Segmentation models often output masks at a lower resolution than the input image. If you’re not upsampling correctly, your masks will be jagged or misaligned.

Diffusion negative prompts and guidance. Setting guidance scale too high produces oversaturated, artifact-ridden images. Setting it too low produces blurry, prompt-ignoring images. Tune per model and per use case.

VLM hallucinations. Always validate VLM outputs against ground truth or an independent checker, especially for counting, spatial reasoning, and OCR-like tasks. A confident VLM is not a correct one.

Tracking ID switches. Trackers mis-link identities across occlusions. If your downstream system depends on stable IDs (e.g., counting unique customers), monitor switch rate explicitly.

3D reconstruction camera poses. Both NeRF and Gaussian Splatting require accurate camera poses for input images; bad poses give bad reconstructions. Use COLMAP or hloc to estimate poses unless your capture rig provides them.

Edge model quantization quality regression. Some operators (LayerNorm, GELU) quantize poorly to int8 with naive PTQ. Always benchmark accuracy after quantization on your real data, not on a benchmark.

Embedding-index staleness. Re-embed your image library when you upgrade the encoder. Mixing old and new embeddings in the same FAISS index is a silent correctness bug.

Diffusion fine-tuning data quality. A LoRA or DreamBooth fine-tune learns whatever’s in your training images, including artifacts you didn’t notice. Curate aggressively.

Real-time pipeline latency budget. Sum your per-stage latencies before assuming the whole pipeline meets your target. End-to-end is what the user sees.

Where this goes next

Vision is, increasingly, not a separate field. Detection feeds VLMs. Segmentation feeds diffusion editors. Depth feeds video generation. Retrieval feeds RAG systems. Most of this chapter’s models are components in larger systems that are themselves components in still-larger ones.

Chapter 10 turns to NLP: the field that paralleled vision through the deep-learning era and ended up in the same place transformers everywhere, foundation models pretrained on enormous corpora, a small set of recipes that ship most of production. Chapter 11 covers the embedding side of NLP that becomes the bridge to LLMs. Chapter 12 covers speech and audio, the last classical-era field. Then Volume 1 ends and Volume 2 picks up at the transformer itself, which is where the lines between vision, language, audio, and reasoning blur for good.

If a future chapter assumes “you know what a detector or a diffusion model or a VLM is doing under the hood,” this is the chapter to come back to.

Open Chapter 10 when you’re ready.

Chapter 10 NLP Before Transformers

In 2014, a graduate student trained an English-to-French translator by hand. Two GRU networks one to read English, one to write French tied together by a single hidden state. The translator worked beautifully on short sentences. As soon as the input passed about 30 words, output quality fell off a cliff. The model couldn’t remember the start of a sentence by the time it had to translate the end.

Bahdanau, Cho, and Bengio published a three-line fix later that year. Instead of compressing the entire input into one fixed-size vector, let the decoder look back at every input position whenever it generates an output word. Read the source again, every step. They called it “attention.” Three years later, a different paper threw away the recurrence entirely, kept only the attention, and named the resulting architecture the Transformer. Modern AI begins there.

This chapter is the world before that pivot. It is also, despite the framing, useful most of the techniques in here still ship in production. Tokenizers, TF-IDF, word embeddings, n-gram language models, sequence-to-sequence with attention: every transformer-based system in 2026 is built on top of some subset of these ideas. The job here is to build them yourself, in roughly the order they were invented, so that when Volume 2 introduces the transformer, the only new thing in the room will be the architecture, not the vocabulary.

We will go from raw text to learned representations to neural sequence models, and we will end at the doorstep of the transformer. Eighteen lessons, one chapter, one voice.

Why this chapter exists

You can use a modern LLM API every day for years and never need to know what a tokenizer is doing under the hood. Right up until something breaks a model returns 500 unexpected tokens, an embedding is computed against the wrong vocabulary, a multilingual prompt costs five times more than its English equivalent and then you need to know exactly what’s happening at the byte-pair-encoding layer.

Pre-transformer NLP teaches that layer. It also teaches the intuition for what a word, a sentence, and a sequence look like to a neural network. Most of the conceptual machinery you’ll meet in Volume 2 embeddings, context, attention, sequence-to-sequence, classifier heads on top of language models was invented in the pre-transformer era and inherited wholesale. Skip this chapter and Volume 2 will read like magic; do this chapter and it’ll read like engineering.

Text processing: turning sentences into numbers

The first thing a computer needs is for the text to be numbers. A sentence is a string; a model wants tensors. Everything else in NLP follows from how you cross that gap.

Tokenization

A token is a unit of text. The simplest tokenizer splits on whitespace: "the cat sat" becomes ["the", "cat", "sat"]. That gets you halfway, then breaks on punctuation ("don't" should arguably be one token, two tokens, or three depending on the downstream task), contractions, hyphens, URLs, emojis, code, and every language other than English.

Three eras of tokenization:

Word tokenization. Whitespace + punctuation rules. Easy to implement; struggles with morphologically rich languages. NLTK and spaCy ship robust regex-based word tokenizers; for any classical NLP task in English they are perfectly fine.

import nltk
nltk.tokenize.word_tokenize("Don't think it's a problem!")
# ['Do', "n't", 'think', 'it', "'s", 'a', 'problem', '!']

Character tokenization. Each character is a token. Vocabulary is tiny (a few hundred symbols); sequences are very long; the model has to learn what a word is from scratch. Used in some early neural models and in specialized domains (e.g., handwriting, low-resource languages).

Subword tokenization (the modern boring default). Common words stay whole; rare words break into smaller pieces. "unhappiness" becomes ["un", "happi", "ness"]. The vocabulary is fixed (typically 30k-100k pieces); any string is representable as a sequence of pieces; the model learns reusable morphology.

Three subword algorithms have won. Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) starts from characters and greedily merges the most frequent adjacent pair until the vocabulary reaches the target size. WordPiece does the same but optimizes a likelihood-based score. SentencePiece treats whitespace as just another character and works language-agnostically. GPT and Llama use byte-level BPE; BERT uses WordPiece; T5 and Llama 2 use SentencePiece.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
print(tokenizer.tokenize("unhappiness"))   # ['un', 'h', 'appiness']
print(tokenizer.encode("unhappiness"))     # [403, 71, 1324, 1108]

Subword tokenizers are why an LLM never produces an “out-of-vocabulary” error; every byte in any string can be represented. We come back to tokenization in Volume 2 because it is the source of half the surprising LLM behaviors you’ll encounter.

Normalization

Once you have tokens, you usually want to normalize them. Lowercasing collapses "The" and "the" into one token (saves vocabulary space; loses case information that sometimes matters). Unicode normalization (NFC/NFKC) makes “café” with one accented character match “café” with a base “e” plus a combining accent. Punctuation handling decides whether !?. are kept, dropped, or merged.

For classical NLP, the standard pre-processing is “lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric, collapse whitespace.” For modern transformer-based systems, you typically don’t normalize at all the model learns from the raw text.

Stopwords, stemming, lemmatization

Stopwords are common words (“the”, “a”, “is”, “of”) that classical bag-of-words methods drop because they don’t carry topic information. Modern dense models keep them; they’re cheap and sometimes carry signal.

Stemming chops off suffixes by rule: "running" → "run", "happily" → "happili". Crude but fast. Porter’s algorithm is the boring default.

Lemmatization maps to a dictionary form: "running" → "run", "better" → "good". Smarter, slower, requires a lexicon. spaCy’s lemmatizer is the boring default.

In 2026, you rarely use stemming or lemmatization for new systems subword tokenization already handles morphology, and the loss of information from stemming hurts more than it helps. They survive in legacy search systems and rules-based linguistic analysis.

Bag-of-words and TF-IDF

The simplest way to turn a document into a vector: count the words.

A bag-of-words representation is a vector where each entry counts how many times a vocabulary word appears in the document. If your vocabulary has 50,000 words and your document has 200 of them, you get a 50,000-dim vector with 200 nonzero entries. Sparse, simple, surprisingly effective for many text classification tasks.

The problem with raw counts: common words (“the”, “of”, “is”) swamp informative ones (“transformer”, “thrombocytopenia”, “rendezvous”). TF-IDF corrects for this by weighting each word by how informative it is.

tf-idf(w,d)=\underbracecount(w,d)∣d∣term frequency⋅\underbracelogN∣{d′:w∈d′}∣inverse document frequency\text{tf-idf}(w, d) = \underbrace{\frac{\text{count}(w, d)}{|d|}}_{\text{term frequency}} \cdot \underbrace{\log \frac{N}{|\{d': w \in d'\}|}}_{\text{inverse document frequency}} tf-idf(w,d)=\underbrace​∣d∣​​count(w,d)​​​term frequency​​⋅\underbracelog​∣{d​′​​:w∈d​′​​}∣​​N​​​inverse document frequency​​

The first factor term frequency measures how often a word appears in the document. The second inverse document frequency measures how rare the word is across the corpus. Their product is high when a word is frequent in this document and rare in the corpus overall. “Transformer” in a deep-learning paper scores high. “the” scores near zero in any document.

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer

corpus = [
    "the cat sat on the mat",
    "the dog chased the cat",
    "transformers learn from text",
]
vectorizer = TfidfVectorizer()
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(corpus)
print(X.shape)                       # (3, 12)   3 docs, 12 unique words
print(vectorizer.get_feature_names_out())

TF-IDF + a linear classifier (logistic regression, linear SVM) is the boring default for any text classification problem with under 100k labeled examples. It trains in seconds, predicts in microseconds, and is hard to beat without much more data. In 2026, the production layout for “I have 5,000 labeled customer-support tickets and need to route them” is still TF-IDF + logistic regression, with a transformer-based model only when the simple recipe falls short.

For information retrieval (search), the TF-IDF cousin BM25 is the unfair workhorse: it adds document-length normalization and saturation terms to TF-IDF and remains the strongest single keyword-search baseline you can build. Most “hybrid search” systems in 2026 (BM25 + dense embeddings) are TF-IDF’s spiritual descendant on one half of the pipeline.

TF-IDF from scratch, in the simplest terms

To make the concepts above tangible, here’s the entire algorithm in 30 lines, no library:

import math
from collections import Counter

def tokenize(text):
    return text.lower().split()

def tf(doc):
    counts = Counter(doc)
    total = len(doc)
    return {w: c / total for w, c in counts.items()}

def idf(corpus_tokens):
    N = len(corpus_tokens)
    df = Counter()
    for doc in corpus_tokens:
        for w in set(doc):
            df[w] += 1
    return {w: math.log(N / count) for w, count in df.items()}

def tfidf(corpus):
    tokens = [tokenize(d) for d in corpus]
    idf_table = idf(tokens)
    return [{w: tf_val * idf_table[w] for w, tf_val in tf(doc).items()} for doc in tokens]

corpus = [
    "the cat sat on the mat",
    "the dog chased the cat",
    "transformers learn from text",
]
for i, doc_vec in enumerate(tfidf(corpus)):
    top = sorted(doc_vec.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:3]
    print(f"doc {i}: {top}")

What you’ll see when you run it: in document 0, the highest-scoring words are mat, sat, and on exactly the words that distinguish this document from the others. The word the, despite appearing twice, gets near-zero score because every document contains it. That’s the IDF term doing its job. This 30-line script is, conceptually, what every keyword-search engine in the world has been doing for forty years.

Word embeddings: words as vectors

TF-IDF treats words as separate, unrelated symbols. The word “king” and the word “queen” are as far apart as “king” and “carburetor.” This is wrong. The 2013 idea that fixed it word embeddings is one of the most important developments in NLP, and it set the conceptual foundation that every later model inherits.

The premise: train a model to predict words from their context, and the parameters of that model end up encoding semantic meaning. Words that appear in similar contexts (“doctor”, “nurse”) get similar vectors. The model never sees a “meaning” label; it learns meaning indirectly, by trying to fill in blanks.

Word2Vec

Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) trains a shallow neural network with one hidden layer. The vocabulary is mapped to embeddings, and the model is asked to predict context words from a center word (skip-gram) or a center word from its context (CBOW). After training, the embedding matrix one vector per vocabulary word is the artifact you keep.

from gensim.models import Word2Vec

sentences = [["the", "cat", "sat"], ["the", "dog", "ran"], ["a", "cat", "ran"]]
model = Word2Vec(sentences, vector_size=100, window=5, min_count=1, sg=1)  # sg=1 = skip-gram
print(model.wv["cat"])       # 100-dim vector
print(model.wv.most_similar("cat"))

The famous Word2Vec example is king − man + woman ≈ queen. The embedding space turns out to encode analogies as vector arithmetic, even though no one trained it to. Linguistic regularities gender, plurality, country-capital fall out for free.

To see why this is surprising, run the analogy:

import gensim.downloader as api
vectors = api.load("glove-wiki-gigaword-100")  # 400k pretrained 100-dim vectors
result = vectors.most_similar(positive=["king", "woman"], negative=["man"], topn=3)
print(result)
# [('queen', 0.78), ('monarch', 0.69), ('throne', 0.68)]

Nothing in the training procedure said “the gender axis lives in this direction.” The model just predicted words from context, on a couple of billion words of text. The geometry emerged. This was the early-2010s evidence that distributional semantics you shall know a word by the company it keeps, as the linguist J.R. Firth put it in 1957 actually works at scale. Every embedding-based system since (CLIP for images and text, embedding-based recommenders, retrieval-augmented LLMs) is a direct descendant of this single observation.

Two training tricks make Word2Vec scale. Negative sampling: instead of computing softmax over the entire vocabulary at every step (expensive), sample a few random “negative” words and train the model to assign low scores to them. Hierarchical softmax is an alternative that organizes the vocabulary in a binary tree, with similar effect. Both are now historical curiosities, but they’re the same engineering idea (avoid the expensive softmax) that recurs in modern contrastive learning.

GloVe and fastText

GloVe (Pennington et al., 2014, Stanford) takes a different angle: factorize a co-occurrence matrix directly. For each pair of words, count how often they appear within a window of each other. Then learn embeddings ui,vju_i, v_ju​i​​,v​j​​ such that ui⋅vj≈logP(wj∣wi)u_i \cdot v_j \approx \log P(w_j \mid w_i)u​i​​⋅v​j​​≈logP(w​j​​∣w​i​​). In practice, GloVe and Word2Vec produce embeddings of similar quality; GloVe trains slightly differently and is sometimes preferred for downstream tasks.

fastText (Bojanowski et al., 2016, Facebook) extends Word2Vec to subword embeddings. Each word’s vector is the sum of its character n-gram embeddings. The result: the model handles out-of-vocabulary words by composing them from pieces, and it works better on morphologically rich languages where Word2Vec struggles.

In 2026, all three are mostly historical. The 2019-onward shift to contextual embeddings (BERT, then GPT-style) made static word embeddings obsolete for most tasks. They survive as: pre-trained vectors for low-resource scenarios, baselines in research, and the conceptual foundation for every embedding-based system you’ll meet later.

What an embedding actually is

Stripped of magic: an embedding is a learned vector that represents something. The “thing” can be a word (Word2Vec), a sentence (Sentence-BERT, in Volume 2), a document (Doc2Vec), an image (CLIP), an entity (knowledge graph embeddings), or anything else you can train a model to put into a similarity-respecting space.

The geometric property that makes embeddings useful similar things have similar vectors, so nearest-neighbor lookup is semantic search is the foundation of half of modern AI. Recommendation systems, retrieval-augmented generation, content moderation, deduplication, similarity search, retrieval, vector databases all of them are embedding-plus-nearest-neighbor.

Text classification: sentiment, topics, intents

Once you have a vector representation of a document TF-IDF, averaged word embeddings, or anything else text classification reduces to standard machine learning. Most production text classifiers in 2026 are still some flavor of “vectorize the text, run a classifier.”

Sentiment analysis is the canonical example: positive vs. negative (binary), or a 5-star scale (ordinal), or fine-grained emotion (multi-class). The classical recipe is TF-IDF + logistic regression, which gets ~85% accuracy on movie reviews with no fuss. Modern systems use pretrained transformers fine-tuned on labeled data; they reach 95%+ but cost more.

Aspect-based sentiment analysis is more nuanced: not just “this review is positive” but “the food was good, the service was bad.” The output is a (aspect, sentiment) tuple per sentence. Classical pipelines extract aspects with a noun-phrase chunker and classify sentiment per aspect; modern systems use a single fine-tuned transformer that does both.

Intent classification (used in chatbots and voice assistants) is the same structural problem with different labels: “play music,” “set timer,” “ask weather.” The classes are pre-defined; the model picks one.

For all of these, the boring default in 2026 is: TF-IDF + linear classifier as a strong baseline, then a fine-tuned transformer (BERT-base, DeBERTa, or a small Llama) when you can afford it. We meet the transformer fine-tuning recipe in Volume 2.

Sequence labeling: NER and POS tagging

Some NLP tasks output one label per token, not one per document. Two have shaped the field.

Part-of-speech (POS) tagging assigns each word a grammatical category noun, verb, adjective, etc. "The cat sat on the mat" becomes "DET NOUN VERB ADP DET NOUN". POS tags are an input feature for higher-level NLP tasks (parsing, IE, MT) and a useful smoke test for any new tokenizer or model.

Named Entity Recognition (NER) tags spans of text as belonging to categories: person, organization, location, date, money. "Tim Cook visited Microsoft in Redmond on Tuesday" produces [("Tim Cook", PER), ("Microsoft", ORG), ("Redmond", LOC), ("Tuesday", DATE)].

The classical formulation for both is sequence labeling with a BIO (or BIOES) tag scheme: each token gets a label like B-PER (beginning of person), I-PER (inside person), or O (outside any entity). Models output per-token labels, which a post-processing step glues into spans.

The classical models Hidden Markov Models (HMMs), Conditional Random Fields (CRFs), structured perceptrons were eventually replaced by neural sequence models (BiLSTM-CRFs, then BERT-based taggers). Today the boring default is a fine-tuned transformer with a token-classification head:

from transformers import pipeline
ner = pipeline("ner", model="dslim/bert-base-NER", aggregation_strategy="simple")
ner("Tim Cook visited Microsoft in Redmond on Tuesday.")

For non-English NER, spaCy ships strong off-the-shelf models in 70+ languages. For domain-specific NER (medical, legal, biomedical), fine-tune on labeled data usually a few thousand examples is enough.

Parsing recovering grammatical structure (constituency trees or dependency trees) was a major NLP subfield. It’s still useful for explaining language; it’s mostly absent from modern downstream pipelines because LLMs absorb the function of parsing implicitly. spaCy ships a fast dependency parser; the Stanford CoreNLP project ships everything else. We mention parsing for completeness; you will rarely need it in 2026.

Neural sequence models: CNNs and RNNs for text

Once words are vectors, you can build neural models over sequences of vectors. Two architectures defined the pre-transformer era.

CNNs for text

Yes, convolutions work on text. Treat the embedding matrix of a sentence as a 1-D image (sequence × embedding-dim) and apply 1-D convolutions. Each filter learns to detect a specific n-gram pattern; max-pooling over the sequence picks the strongest activation regardless of position.

TextCNN (Kim, 2014) trained convolutional layers on top of word embeddings for sentence classification. It was simple, fast, and beat most prior baselines. In 2026 TextCNN is mostly historical fine-tuned BERT does better but the idea recurs: 1-D convs over sequences are still used in audio, in some efficient long-context language models, and in low-latency edge text classifiers.

RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs

The natural sequence model for text is a Recurrent Neural Network: process one token at a time, maintain a hidden state, update the hidden state at each step.

ht=σ(Wht−1+Uxt+b)h_t = \sigma(W h_{t-1} + U x_t + b) h​t​​=σ(Wh​t−1​​+Ux​t​​+b)

The vanilla RNN had two problems. Vanishing gradients: gradients shrunk exponentially through time, so the model couldn’t learn long-range dependencies. Exploding gradients: occasionally they did the opposite. The fix was gating: explicit, learned controls for what to keep, what to forget, and what to write to the hidden state.

LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory, Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) introduced three gates input, forget, output and a separate cell state that flowed through the sequence with minimal interference. The forget gate’s job was to learn what to forget, which was unintuitive but turned out to be the key insight: a network that can selectively prune its memory can keep useful long-range information.

In simple terms, an LSTM cell is a small machine with two memories: the hidden state (working memory, what the network outputs) and the cell state (long-term memory, what flows through with minimal changes). At each step, three gates control the cell:

  • The forget gate looks at the current input and previous hidden state and decides “of what’s in long-term memory, what should I delete?” Its output is a vector of values between 0 (forget) and 1 (keep) that multiplies the cell state element-wise.
  • The input gate decides “what new information should I write into long-term memory?” Combined with a candidate-content vector, it adds new content.
  • The output gate decides “what should I expose to the rest of the network this step?” It selects which parts of the cell state become the new hidden state.

The trick is that the cell state has a direct path through time it gets multiplied by the forget gate and added to by the input gate, but no transformation forces information to vanish. Gradients can flow back through arbitrarily many steps without exponential decay. That single architectural change made it possible to learn dependencies hundreds of tokens apart.

GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit, Cho et al., 2014) simplified the LSTM down to two gates (reset and update) and merged the cell and hidden states. Comparable performance, fewer parameters, faster training. In practice the choice between LSTM and GRU is taste; GRU is the slight default for speed.

import torch.nn as nn

class TextRNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, embed_dim, hidden_dim, num_classes):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
        self.rnn   = nn.GRU(embed_dim, hidden_dim, batch_first=True, bidirectional=True)
        self.fc    = nn.Linear(hidden_dim * 2, num_classes)

    def forward(self, x):
        emb = self.embed(x)                  # (B, T, E)
        out, _ = self.rnn(emb)               # (B, T, 2H)
        pooled = out.mean(dim=1)             # average over time
        return self.fc(pooled)

A bidirectional GRU over word embeddings, with a mean-pool and a linear head, was the workhorse of NLP from 2015 to 2018. Half the production text classifiers and sequence labelers of that era look exactly like the snippet above. Replace embed with a pretrained Word2Vec lookup, and you have a model that holds its own on most classification tasks.

Why RNNs lost to transformers

Two structural problems. Sequential computation: an RNN processes tokens one at a time, so you can’t parallelize across the sequence axis. On a GPU, this leaves most of the hardware idle. Long-range dependencies: even with LSTM gating, vanishing gradients return at long enough sequences. Transformers fix both at once: they parallelize across the sequence (every position attends to every other in O(1) wall-clock time per layer) and they have direct connections between distant positions (no “forgetting” through time).

RNNs and their variants are still used. They’re the right choice when memory is tight (an LSTM is much smaller than a transformer of comparable expressive power), when latency matters more than throughput (autoregressive RNN inference can be faster than transformer inference for very long sequences in some regimes), and in specific domains like time-series forecasting where the temporal prior matters. But for general NLP, transformers won.

Sequence-to-sequence and attention

Most NLP tasks pre-2017 took the form: input sequence → output sequence. Translation: English → French. Summarization: long article → short summary. Dialogue: user turn → system turn. Question answering (in some framings): question → answer.

The encoder-decoder architecture (Sutskever et al., 2014; Cho et al., 2014) handled these uniformly. An encoder RNN read the input sequence and produced a final hidden state. A decoder RNN took that state as initialization and generated the output one token at a time, using its own hidden state and its previous output token.

input:    The  cat  sat  on  the  mat
encoder:  GRU step → step → step → step → step → final hidden state h
                                                                        |
decoder:                                                              GRU init=h
                                                                        |
output:   Le  chat  s'est  assis  sur  le  tapis

It worked. It also broke at long inputs, because everything the encoder learned had to fit in one fixed-size vector. Translate a one-sentence message: fine. Translate a paragraph: the model forgot the start by the time it had to produce the end.

Attention: the fix that broke the bottleneck

The Bahdanau attention mechanism (2014) is now famous as the predecessor of the transformer. The fix in plain English: don’t make the decoder squint at one summary vector. Keep all the encoder’s hidden states. At each decoder step, ask “which encoder positions should I look at right now?” Compute a weighted average of encoder states, with the weights learned per step. Use that weighted average as additional input to the decoder.

In math, at decoder step ttt:

  1. Score each encoder position: et,i=score(st−1,hi)e_{t,i} = \text{score}(s_{t-1}, h_i)e​t,i​​=score(s​t−1​​,h​i​​) where st−1s_{t-1}s​t−1​​ is the previous decoder state and hih_ih​i​​ is the iii-th encoder state.
  2. Normalize to weights: αt,i=softmax(et,i)\alpha_{t,i} = \text{softmax}(e_{t,i})α​t,i​​=softmax(e​t,i​​).
  3. Compute the context vector: ct=∑iαt,ihic_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} h_ic​t​​=∑​i​​α​t,i​​h​i​​.
  4. Use ctc_tc​t​​ alongside the previous decoder state and output token to produce the next output.

The score function comes in two flavors. Bahdanau (additive): et,i=v⊤tanh(Wast−1+Uahi)e_{t,i} = v^\top \tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a h_i)e​t,i​​=v​⊤​​tanh(W​a​​s​t−1​​+U​a​​h​i​​), with three learnable matrices. Luong (dot-product): et,i=st⊤hie_{t,i} = s_t^\top h_ie​t,i​​=s​t​⊤​​h​i​​, requiring matched dimensions. Modern transformers use a scaled variant of dot-product attention; the structural idea is the same.

Plot the attention weights over time and you see the model aligning output tokens to input tokens soft, learned attention is essentially soft, learned alignment. For machine translation, the attention matrix is a heatmap that shows the model translating “Je” → “I”, “ne…pas” → “do not”, and so on. This was the moment NLP became interpretable, sort of.

def additive_attention(s_prev, encoder_states, W_a, U_a, v_a):
    proj_dec = W_a @ s_prev                    # (d_attn,)
    proj_enc = encoder_states @ U_a.T          # (T, d_attn)
    e = (np.tanh(proj_enc + proj_dec)) @ v_a   # (T,)
    alpha = softmax(e)                         # (T,)
    context = alpha @ encoder_states           # (d_h,)
    return context, alpha

Once you have the attention machinery encoder states + a query + a similarity score + a softmax + a weighted average the path to the transformer is purely architectural. Throw out the RNN entirely. Make every token attend to every other token. Run it in parallel. Stack 12 layers. Add residual connections and LayerNorm. That’s the 2017 paper.

A worked attention example, in concrete numbers

To make attention completely concrete, here’s a four-token toy. Imagine the encoder has produced four 3-dimensional hidden states for the words cat, sat, on, mat:

import numpy as np

H = np.array([
    [1.0, 0.0, 0.2],   # cat
    [0.5, 0.4, 0.1],   # sat
    [0.0, 0.2, 0.9],   # on
    [0.2, 0.9, 0.3],   # mat
])

def softmax(x):
    e = np.exp(x - x.max())
    return e / e.sum()

The decoder is about to produce the French word chat (cat). Its current state is a 3-dim vector that looks “cat-like”:

s = np.array([0.9, 0.1, 0.2])
scores  = H @ s                    # dot-product attention
weights = softmax(scores)
context = weights @ H

print("scores: ", scores.round(2))
# [0.94 0.51 0.20 0.33]
print("weights:", weights.round(2))
# [0.42 0.27 0.20 0.20]
print("context:", context.round(2))
# [0.51 0.32 0.34]

The model assigned 42% of its attention to position 0 (cat), with the rest spread thin across the other words. The context vector is mostly the cat hidden state, mixed slightly with the others. When the decoder needs to produce the next word say s'est, the verb-aux its state will shift, the scores will redistribute toward sat, and the context will follow.

That single mechanism query, score, softmax, weighted average is all of attention. The transformer is what happens when you apply it ten thousand times in parallel and call the source-and-target the same sequence.

Machine translation, summarization, QA, IR

The big four pre-transformer NLP tasks. Each one is a sequence-to-sequence problem with attention, with task-specific tweaks.

Machine Translation. Statistical machine translation (phrase-based, IBM models) ruled until 2014, when Bahdanau showed neural models could compete. By 2016, neural MT (encoder-decoder with attention) overtook statistical MT in production at Google, Facebook, and Microsoft. The 2017 transformer (Vaswani et al., explicitly motivated by translation) made it definitive. By 2026, every production translator is transformer-based; classical MT survives in low-resource languages and as a baseline.

Text summarization comes in two flavors. Extractive summarization picks sentences from the input verbatim. The classical approach (TextRank, LexRank) treats each sentence as a node in a graph, with edges weighted by sentence similarity, and runs PageRank. Surprisingly effective, fast, deterministic. Abstractive summarization generates new sentences. Pre-transformer abstractive summarizers (pointer networks, encoder-decoder with copy mechanisms) struggled with hallucination and factuality. Modern abstractive summarization (BART, T5, GPT) dominates; we cover it in Volume 2.

Question answering has two main forms. Extractive QA: given a passage and a question, find the span in the passage that answers it (SQuAD). The classical formulation predicts start and end token positions; modern extractive QA is BERT with a span-classification head. Open-domain QA: given just a question, retrieve relevant documents and extract an answer. The “retrieve then read” pattern from the pre-transformer era is the conceptual ancestor of modern RAG (Volume 2).

Information Retrieval and Search is the granddaddy of NLP. The classical pipeline: tokenize, build an inverted index, score with BM25, optionally rerank. Modern search adds a dense-embedding stage (encode query and documents with a sentence transformer, score by cosine similarity) and combines the two (“hybrid search”). The boring default in 2026 is BM25 + dense embeddings + reranker, and we’ll see it again in Volume 2’s RAG chapters. Elasticsearch and OpenSearch are the production engines that ship this.

Topic modeling

Given a collection of documents, find the topics that recur. A topic is a distribution over words; a document is a distribution over topics. The classical algorithm is Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA, Blei et al., 2003), which learns these distributions by Bayesian inference.

from gensim import corpora, models

texts = [["machine", "learning", "algorithm"],
         ["deep", "learning", "neural"],
         ["coffee", "morning", "espresso"]]
dictionary = corpora.Dictionary(texts)
corpus = [dictionary.doc2bow(t) for t in texts]
lda = models.LdaModel(corpus, num_topics=2, id2word=dictionary)
for topic_id, words in lda.print_topics():
    print(topic_id, words)

LDA is interpretable but unstable different runs find different topics, and finding the “right” number of topics is more art than science. In 2026 the boring default is BERTopic: cluster sentence-transformer embeddings of your documents, then describe each cluster by the words most distinctive to it. Faster, more interpretable, and works on shorter text where LDA fails.

Topic modeling is one of the few NLP areas where transformers haven’t fully replaced the classical recipe partly because LDA’s interpretability is genuinely useful, partly because BERTopic’s clustering-on-embeddings approach is itself just a clever wrapper around modern transformer embeddings.

Pre-transformer text generation

Generative text models existed before transformers. Two families.

N-gram language models assign probabilities to sequences of words by chaining Markov assumptions: P(wt∣w1:t−1)≈P(wt∣wt−N+1:t−1)P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1}) \approx P(w_t \mid w_{t-N+1:t-1})P(w​t​​∣w​1:t−1​​)≈P(w​t​​∣w​t−N+1:t−1​​). A 3-gram model uses the previous two words to predict the next. Trained by counting; smoothed by techniques like Kneser-Ney to handle unseen n-grams. Surprisingly effective, fast to train, runs on a laptop, and is still competitive for keyboard autocomplete and certain very low-latency applications. Modern LLMs are conceptual descendants they’re n-gram models with arbitrarily-large context and learned (rather than counted) probabilities.

RNN-based language models generated text by feeding each step’s output back as the next step’s input. Karpathy’s 2015 “Unreasonable Effectiveness of RNNs” demo trained character-level LSTMs to produce convincing-ish Shakespeare and code. The samples were locally fluent but globally incoherent pre-transformer text generation could produce plausible-looking sentences but not plausible-looking paragraphs.

The path from RNN language models to GPT is short. Replace the RNN with a transformer decoder. Scale up. The “language model that generates by predicting the next token” idea is identical; only the architecture changed.

Chatbots: rules, retrieval, generation

A historical view of chatbots tells you everything about how NLP evolved.

Rule-based (1960s-2010s): hand-coded patterns. ELIZA matched simple regexes. AIML powered a generation of chatbots with <pattern> and <template> rules. Rule-based bots are predictable, cheap, and brittle. They’re still used for narrow, high-stakes flows (banking, scheduling) where you cannot afford a hallucinated answer.

Retrieval-based (2010s): given a user message, retrieve the most similar question from a database of (question, answer) pairs and return its answer. TF-IDF for similarity, then dense embeddings. Retrieval bots can’t say anything new; they can only repeat what’s in their database. That’s a feature, not a bug, for FAQ systems.

Generative (pre-transformer): seq2seq models trained on (turn, response) pairs. They generated novel responses, but the responses were generic (“I don’t know,” “OK,”) because the model collapsed onto safe high-probability outputs. Useless for production.

Generative (transformer-era, 2018+): every modern chatbot is a fine-tuned LLM. The pre-transformer dream of a model that handles arbitrary user input fluently is now real. Volume 2 covers the architecture, Volume 3 covers production deployment, and Phase 14 (later in Volume 2) covers the agent layer that wraps a chatbot with tools.

The thing to take away: every kind of chatbot still ships somewhere. The choice is not “rules vs LLMs” but “which mechanism for which slot of the conversation.” Hybrid systems rules for high-stakes intents, retrieval for FAQ, generative for everything else are the boring default for production assistants.

Multilingual NLP

Most of what we’ve discussed is English-centric, partly because English-language data dominates the internet, partly because the field’s literature is English. The reality of production NLP is multilingual.

Three eras.

Per-language pipelines. Train one model per language. spaCy ships pipelines for 70+ languages this way. Works; expensive to maintain.

Multilingual word embeddings. Multilingual fastText, then MUSE (Conneau et al., 2017), trained word embeddings in many languages and aligned them so that “cat” and “chat” had similar vectors. Cross-lingual transfer became possible: train on English data, predict on French data.

Multilingual transformers. mBERT, XLM-R, and mDeBERTa are pretrained on text in 100+ languages with shared subword vocabulary. They produce contextual embeddings that work across languages out of the box. Modern multilingual NLP is “use XLM-R or a multilingual T5; fine-tune on whatever target-language data you have.”

For low-resource languages languages with little training data on the public web the transfer-learning approach is essential. Cross-lingual transfer trains on a high-resource language and fine-tunes on the target. Code-switching (handling sentences that mix languages, common in many global communities) is harder and an active research area.

In 2026, the open-source frontier for multilingual is dominated by Llama 3, Qwen, Mistral, and similar LLMs trained on broad multilingual corpora. They handle hundreds of languages with varying quality; the major ones (Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, Hindi, Arabic, Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Russian) are essentially as good as English for most tasks.

Evaluation in classical NLP

Each task family has its standard metrics.

Classification: accuracy, F1, balanced accuracy, AUC. Same as Chapter 6. Multiclass: macro-F1 (unweighted average) for fairness across classes, micro-F1 (weighted by frequency) for the total picture.

Sequence labeling (NER, POS): token-level accuracy is misleading because most tokens are “O” (outside any entity); use entity-level F1 (an entity counts only if both its span and label match).

Translation: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) compares n-gram overlap between candidate and reference translations. Imperfect it doesn’t capture semantic equivalence but the standard. chrF (character F-score) and METEOR are alternatives. In 2026, COMET (a learned metric using XLM-R) correlates better with human judgment and is the modern preference.

Summarization: ROUGE (n-gram overlap with reference summaries). Same flavor of imperfect as BLEU; supplemented by human evaluation and learned metrics like BERTScore.

Question answering: Exact Match (the predicted answer string equals the reference) and F1 (token-level overlap). For open-domain, MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) and Recall@K evaluate the retrieval step.

Retrieval / search: NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain), Recall@K, MAP, MRR. Choose based on whether you care about the top-1 result (MRR) or the top-K (Recall@K).

A general note: classical NLP metrics correlate weakly with human judgment, especially for generation tasks. By 2024 the field shifted toward LLM-as-a-judge evaluation: ask a strong LLM to rate outputs on a rubric. Volume 2 covers this in depth.

What you’d actually use

A summary of what survives from the pre-transformer era into 2026, by job:

Job Default Why
Tokenization (custom) tokenizers library, BPE Fast, byte-level, handles any text
Tokenization (use a model’s) transformers.AutoTokenizer Always match the model
Text classification (small data) TF-IDF + logistic regression Hard to beat under 10k labels
Text classification (more data) Fine-tuned BERT or DeBERTa Volume 2 territory
NER (off-the-shelf) spaCy 70+ languages, fast
NER (custom) Fine-tuned BERT-NER Few-thousand labeled spans
POS / parsing spaCy Very few production needs
Word embeddings (legacy) fastText When transformers are overkill
Sentence embeddings sentence-transformers The boring default for semantic search
Lexical search BM25 (Elasticsearch / OpenSearch) Strongest single-baseline search
Hybrid search BM25 + dense embeddings + reranker Production-grade
Topic modeling BERTopic Faster and more interpretable than LDA
Multilingual model XLM-R or mDeBERTa Cross-lingual transfer
Translation NLLB or Llama / GPT NLLB for breadth, LLMs for quality
Classical text features TF-IDF, n-gram counts, sentiment lexicons Cheap signals for hybrid pipelines
Sequence model (legacy) BiLSTM in PyTorch Edge deployments where transformers don’t fit
Chatbot baseline Retrieval over FAQ + LLM fallback Cheapest reliable production setup

If you forget everything else: subword tokenization, TF-IDF, word embeddings, BM25, and the encoder-decoder-with-attention pattern are the five ideas that survived from this chapter into the LLM era. Master those and Volume 2 will read like an extension, not a revolution.

Sharp edges

A few of the failure modes this chapter exists to prevent.

Tokenizer mismatch. Loading a BERT model with the wrong tokenizer (or vice versa) produces silently wrong predictions. Always pair AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id) with the same model_id.

Stopword removal where context matters. “Not good” becomes “good” if you drop “not.” Removing stopwords is fine for topic-style tasks but harmful for sentiment.

Vocabulary explosion in TF-IDF. A million-document corpus has a million-word vocabulary; the resulting matrix doesn’t fit in memory. Cap with max_features=50_000 and drop very rare words.

Cosine vs. euclidean for embeddings. Most pretrained embeddings are L2-normalized and meant to be compared with cosine similarity. Using euclidean distance silently gives different rankings.

Length bias in BLEU and ROUGE. Both metrics penalize length differences hard. Make sure your candidate and reference summaries are roughly the same length before comparing.

Imbalanced sequence-labeling classes. “O” tokens overwhelm rare entity classes. Always evaluate at the entity level, not the token level.

Train/test leakage through duplicate documents. Web-scraped corpora are full of near-duplicates. Deduplicate aggressively or your test metrics are inflated.

Multilingual tokenizers and language detection. A multilingual tokenizer applied to Chinese will produce many short tokens; the same text in English produces fewer long ones. This affects context-length budgets and per-language costs.

RNN inference latency. A 1,000-token RNN inference is sequential 1,000 forward steps. If you’re sticking with RNNs for production, consider pruning or distillation.

Topic model instability. Different LDA runs with different seeds find different topics. Average across runs or use BERTopic for more stable results.

Where this goes next

Chapter 11 picks up where this chapter left off and crosses into the LLM era proper: subword tokenization in detail, modern embedding models, structured outputs and constrained decoding, the “RAG-shaped” tasks (chunking, retrieval, evaluation) that connect classical NLP to generative AI. Chapter 12 covers speech and audio, the parallel field where many of the same ideas applied.

After Chapter 12, Volume 1 ends. Volume 2 opens at the transformer itself the architecture that rewrote every section of this chapter. By then you’ll have seen the mechanisms (attention, embeddings, sequence-to-sequence) in their pre-transformer form; meeting them again in the transformer setting will be recognition rather than learning.

Open Chapter 11 when you’re ready.

Chapter 11 NLP Building Blocks for the LLM Era

A team building a customer-support assistant in 2026 has the LLM part figured out. They picked a model, wrote a system prompt, hooked up the API, and got a chatbot that produces fluent answers about almost everything except the company’s actual products, policies, and ticket history. To fix that they bolted on retrieval-augmented generation: a vector database of documentation chunks, a search step before each LLM call, the retrieved chunks pasted into the prompt as context. The chatbot got better. It also started hallucinating differently confidently misquoting the manual, citing pages that didn’t exist, returning answers that contradicted the retrieved context.

The fix turned out not to be a better LLM. It was better chunking. The retrieved chunks were too long, too short, or split across the wrong paragraph boundaries; the embedding model wasn’t tuned for the domain; the evaluation pipeline scored the system on exact-match against gold answers, which missed every semantically-correct-but-textually-different response. Once the team fixed those, accuracy doubled. The LLM hadn’t changed.

This chapter is about that layer the NLP building blocks that sit between raw text and a working LLM application. Subword tokenization, modern embedding models, chunking, retrieval, structured outputs, evaluation: the techniques that aren’t the LLM but make the LLM useful. Eleven source lessons, one chapter, one voice. Most of them apply directly to RAG, agents, and every LLM-shaped pipeline you’ll ship.

Why this chapter exists

Volume 2 is about LLMs. This chapter is what you need around LLMs the supporting cast that turns a raw model into a system. None of these topics are the kind of thing that shows up in a flagship paper, but every one of them shows up in every production LLM stack.

If Chapter 10 was the world before the transformer, Chapter 11 is the world the transformer rebuilt. Tokenization moved from rule-based word splitters to byte-level BPE because that’s what LLMs need.
