Embeddings moved from static Word2Vec to contextual sentence-transformers because RAG needs them. Search moved from BM25-only to hybrid because dense embeddings finally got cheap enough. Evaluation moved from BLEU to LLM-as-judge because n-gram metrics couldn’t keep up with generative output. Every shift makes sense once you see the shape of the LLM applications driving it.

By the end of this chapter, you’ll be able to read any modern RAG architecture diagram and know what every box is doing, even before Volume 2 explains the model in the middle.

Subword tokenization in detail

Chapter 10 introduced subword tokenization. This chapter takes it apart, because tokenization is the source of half the surprising LLM behaviors you’ll meet.

Why subword

Word tokenization breaks on rare words and morphology. Character tokenization makes sequences too long. Subword tokenization splits text into pieces common words stay whole, rare words break into smaller subpieces so the vocabulary stays small (typically 30k–100k pieces) but every possible string is representable. There is no “out of vocabulary” error in modern LLMs because every byte of every Unicode string can be encoded as a sequence of subword pieces.

The three canonical algorithms BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece share the same conceptual recipe: start from a small base alphabet, count which adjacent pairs occur most often in the training corpus, merge the most frequent pair into a new token, repeat until you’ve added enough merges to reach the target vocabulary size. The output is a fixed merge table that turns any string into a sequence of pieces.

Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)

The simplest of the three. Originally a compression algorithm (Gage 1994), repurposed for NLP by Sennrich et al. (2016).

def bpe_train(corpus: list[str], num_merges: int) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    vocab = {tuple(word) + ("</w>",): freq for word, freq in count_words(corpus).items()}
    merges = []
    for _ in range(num_merges):
        pairs = pair_counts(vocab)
        if not pairs:
            break
        best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get)
        vocab = merge_pair(best, vocab)
        merges.append(best)
    return merges

The </w> marker is the end-of-word symbol it lets the tokenizer distinguish “low” at the end of a word from “low” at the start of “lowest.” After training, you tokenize by greedily applying the learned merges in order.

GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, and most of the OpenAI lineage use a byte-level BPE variant: instead of operating on Unicode characters, they operate on raw bytes. The base alphabet is 256 (one symbol per byte value). This makes the tokenizer language-agnostic Chinese characters, emoji, code, anything and guarantees that every possible byte string is encodable, no matter how exotic.

WordPiece and SentencePiece

WordPiece (Schuster & Nakajima 2012; used by BERT) replaces BPE’s pair-frequency criterion with a likelihood-based one: pick the merge that maximizes the training-corpus likelihood under a unigram language model. Practically very similar to BPE; the difference shows up in edge cases.

SentencePiece (Kudo & Richardson 2018; used by T5, Llama, Mistral) treats whitespace as just another character (encoded as ▁). This makes it truly language-agnostic many languages don’t use spaces at all (Chinese, Japanese, Thai), and SentencePiece handles them without preprocessing. SentencePiece also supports a unigram algorithm in addition to BPE, which produces slightly different (often better) tokenizations.

In 2026, the boring defaults are: byte-level BPE for GPT-style models, SentencePiece for Llama and most open-source models. You almost never train your own tokenizers and transformers ship the right one for whatever model you’re using.

What goes wrong

Three failure modes worth budgeting for.

Token count surprises. A 100-character English sentence costs ~25 tokens. The same content in Korean might cost 80, in Japanese 100, in Hindi 150. LLM pricing is per-token, which means non-English usage is structurally more expensive. SentencePiece-based multilingual tokenizers are more equitable; English-trained byte-level BPE penalizes everyone else.

Numbers and code. GPT-style tokenizers split numbers in odd ways: 12345 might be [123, 45] or [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] depending on training. This breaks arithmetic. Most modern LLMs train special handling (digit-by-digit tokenization for numbers) to fix it.

The “glitch token” phenomenon. Some tokens in GPT-2’s vocabulary appear so rarely in training data that the model never learned what they mean SolidGoldMagikarp and friends. Prompting with these tokens produces undefined behavior. Modern LLMs filter these out, but the underlying issue (tokens with near-zero training mass) recurs.

The practical advice: when an LLM behavior surprises you, the second thing to check (after the prompt) is the tokenization. tokenizer.tokenize("your input") and look at the pieces.

Modern embedding models

Word2Vec and GloVe gave you static word embeddings one vector per word, regardless of context. Modern embedding models give you sentence embeddings one vector per sentence or paragraph, computed from the full context and they’re what powers retrieval, semantic search, and RAG.

The architecture is straightforward: take a pretrained transformer encoder (BERT-style), pool the output token embeddings into a single vector, fine-tune on a contrastive objective so similar texts produce close vectors. The fine-tuning is what turns a generic encoder into a good retriever; without it, BERT’s [CLS] token is a poor sentence embedding.

Sentence-BERT and the contrastive recipe

Sentence-BERT (Reimers & Gurevych 2019) was the breakthrough. Train a Siamese network on (anchor, positive, negative) triples the anchor and positive should be close in embedding space, the anchor and negative should be far. Loss: triplet loss or contrastive loss. The result was an encoder that produced sentence embeddings 100-1000x faster than BERT’s full pairwise scoring, with comparable quality.

The 2024-2026 boring defaults all descend from this:

Model Size Strength
BGE (large/base/small) 109M-560M Strong general-purpose, MIT license
E5 (Microsoft) 110M-560M Strong on MTEB, easy to fine-tune
Jina v3 570M Long-context (8k tokens), multilingual
Nomic Embed v2 305M Open-source, MoE, long-context
Voyage 3 API High quality, paid, low latency
OpenAI text-embedding-3-large API Production default for OpenAI customers
Cohere Embed v3 API Multilingual + multi-modal

The 2026 boring default is BGE-large for self-hosted, OpenAI text-embedding-3-large or Voyage 3 for API-based. Both produce 1024–3072 dimensional embeddings and are competitive on MTEB (the standard embedding-model benchmark).

Pooling strategies

Once a transformer encoder produces token-level embeddings, you need to pool them into a single vector. Three strategies:

CLS pooling. Take the embedding of the [CLS] token. Standard for BERT classification, mediocre for retrieval without fine-tuning.

Mean pooling. Average all token embeddings, optionally weighted by the attention mask. The boring default for sentence-transformers and most modern models.

Last-token pooling. Take the embedding of the final token. Standard for decoder-only LLMs used as embedders (E5-Mistral, GTE-Qwen). Works because in autoregressive models the last token attends to all previous ones.

Matryoshka and dimensionality reduction

A 1536-dimensional embedding is a lot of storage. Matryoshka representation learning (Kusupati et al. 2022) trains an embedding model so that the first kkk dimensions of a vector are themselves a usable smaller embedding. You can store the first 64 or 256 dimensions and lose only a few percent in retrieval quality, while saving 10-40x on storage and search compute.

OpenAI’s text-embedding-3-large ships natively as a 3072-dim Matryoshka embedding; you truncate to whatever dimension you can afford. Most modern open-source embedders (BGE-M3, Nomic Embed v2) follow.

Chunking strategies for RAG

The single most under-appreciated parameter in any RAG system is how you split documents into chunks. Get it right and a mid-tier embedding model works well; get it wrong and the best embedding model in the world won’t save you. A 2025 Vectara study showed chunking choice influencing retrieval accuracy more than embedding-model choice.

The six strategies

Fixed chunking. Split every NNN characters or tokens, optionally with KKK tokens of overlap. Simple, deterministic, breaks mid-sentence. Good baseline.

Recursive chunking. Try splitting on \n\n first, fall back to \n, then . , then . LangChain’s RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter is the canonical implementation. The 2026 boring default for general-purpose chunking preserves paragraph boundaries when they exist and falls back gracefully when they don’t.

Semantic chunking. Embed each sentence; compute cosine similarity between adjacent sentences; split where similarity drops. Preserves topic coherence. Slower; sometimes produces tiny fragments. Sometimes worse than recursive in benchmarks.

Sentence chunking. Split on sentence boundaries; one or N sentences per chunk. Cheaper than semantic chunking and often comparable.

Parent-document. Store small child chunks for retrieval and larger parent chunks for context. Retrieve by child, return parent. Robust: bad child retrieval still surfaces a reasonable parent.

Late chunking (Jina 2024). Embed the whole document at the token level; pool token embeddings into chunk embeddings after encoding. Preserves cross-chunk context. Requires a long-context embedder.

Contextual retrieval (Anthropic 2024). Prepend each chunk with an LLM-generated description of its position in the document (“This chunk is section 3.2 of the termination clauses…”). Reportedly 35-50% retrieval improvement at indexing-time cost.

The rule that beats every default

Pick your chunk size to match the query type, not the document type:

Query type Chunk size
Factoid (“what is the CEO’s name?”) 256–512 tokens
Analytical / multi-hop 512–1024 tokens
Section-level comprehension 1024–2048 tokens

The chunk should be big enough to contain the answer plus local context, small enough that the retriever’s top-K is focused. Most production RAG runs at 512 tokens with 0–50 token overlap; tune from there.

A common 2024 finding worth flagging: overlap often doesn’t help. The 20% overlap default that everyone uses was not validated; in some benchmarks zero overlap performs identically to 20%. If you’re optimizing storage, try zero overlap and see.

def chunk_recursive(text, size=512, seps=("\n\n", "\n", ". ", " ")):
    if len(text) <= size:
        return [text]
    for sep in seps:
        if sep not in text:
            continue
        parts, chunks, buf = text.split(sep), [], ""
        for p in parts:
            if len(p) > size:
                if buf: chunks.append(buf); buf = ""
                chunks.extend(chunk_recursive(p, size, seps[1:] or (" ",)))
                continue
            cand = (buf + sep + p) if buf else p
            buf, *rest = (cand,) if len(cand) <= size else (p,) + ((buf,) if buf else ())
            if rest: chunks.append(rest[0])
        if buf: chunks.append(buf)
        return [c for c in chunks if c.strip()]
    return [text[i:i+size] for i in range(0, len(text), size)]

That is recursive chunking in 15 lines. Most production RAG starts here.

Information retrieval, modernized

Chapter 10 introduced BM25 and TF-IDF. Modern retrieval pairs them with dense embeddings and a reranker.

Hybrid search: BM25 + dense

Pure dense retrieval (embed the query and documents, search by cosine similarity in a vector index) is great at semantic matching finding documents about the same concept even if they use different words. It’s mediocre at keyword matching exact identifiers, product codes, names. BM25 is the opposite. Neither dominates the other.

Hybrid search runs both in parallel and combines the rankings. Two combination strategies:

Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Sum 1/(k+rankBM25)+1/(k+rankdense)1 / (k + \text{rank}_{\text{BM25}}) + 1 / (k + \text{rank}_{\text{dense}})1/(k+rank​BM25​​)+1/(k+rank​dense​​). Parameter-light, robust, the boring default.

Linear combination. Weight the BM25 and dense scores: score = α * bm25 + (1 - α) * cosine. Requires score normalization; tune α on validation.

def rrf(rankings_a, rankings_b, k=60):
    scores = {}
    for rank, doc_id in enumerate(rankings_a):
        scores[doc_id] = scores.get(doc_id, 0) + 1.0 / (k + rank)
    for rank, doc_id in enumerate(rankings_b):
        scores[doc_id] = scores.get(doc_id, 0) + 1.0 / (k + rank)
    return sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])

In 2026, every serious production RAG system is hybrid. Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Vespa, and Qdrant all ship hybrid out of the box.

Rerankers: the second pass

Hybrid retrieval gives you a top-K list of candidates. A reranker scores each (query, candidate) pair more carefully and re-sorts. Where retrieval is fast and approximate, reranking is slower and exact: the reranker reads the full text of each candidate and the query together and outputs a relevance score.

The architecture is a cross-encoder: feed the query and candidate as a pair into a transformer with a classification head. Slower than bi-encoders (no precomputed embeddings) but much more accurate.

Modern rerankers worth knowing:

  • BGE Reranker v2-m3. Strong open-source default.
  • Cohere Rerank 3. API-based, multilingual.
  • Voyage Reranker 2. API-based, high quality.
  • MS-Marco MiniLM cross-encoders. Tiny, fast, decent.

The full RAG retrieval pipeline in 2026 is: BM25 + dense → top-K (e.g., 50) → rerank → top-N (e.g., 5) → LLM. Each stage trades quality for cost.

Structured outputs and constrained decoding

A modern LLM can write fluent prose. Sometimes you don’t want prose you want JSON, a function call, a SQL query, a code snippet that parses. Getting reliable structured output is its own subfield.

JSON mode and function calling

The cheapest fix is at the API level. OpenAI’s JSON mode, Anthropic’s tool use, and most modern LLM APIs support a response_format={"type": "json_object"} parameter that asks the model to produce valid JSON. The model is fine-tuned to output JSON; the API parses and validates. Combined with a JSON schema describing the expected shape, this is the boring default for “I need structured output” in 2026.

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Extract the date and amount from: invoice for $1,200 dated March 5, 2026"}],
    response_format={
        "type": "json_schema",
        "json_schema": {
            "name": "extraction",
            "schema": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "amount":  {"type": "number"},
                    "date":    {"type": "string", "format": "date"},
                },
                "required": ["amount", "date"],
            },
        },
    },
)

Constrained decoding

When the API doesn’t support what you need, you go to the decoding layer. Constrained decoding restricts the next-token choices to those that maintain validity against a grammar or schema. Three flavors:

Regex/grammar constraints. At each step, mask out logits for tokens that would violate the grammar. The model can only sample valid continuations. Libraries: Outlines, lm-format-enforcer, guidance. Works with any open-source LLM via direct logit manipulation.

Speculative parsing. Generate freely, parse incrementally, backtrack on parse errors. Slower but works with closed APIs.

Output-schema fine-tuning. Fine-tune the model to always produce JSON matching a schema. Most reliable for high-volume production; the cost is one fine-tune per schema.

Choosing a strategy

Need Strategy
Occasional JSON API JSON mode
Strict JSON schema API json_schema + retry on parse failure
Self-hosted, custom grammar Outlines or guidance
Highest reliability, high volume Fine-tune + constrained decoding
Tool/function calling API tool_calls

In 2026 the boring default for “structured output from an LLM” is the API’s structured-output mode plus a Pydantic validation layer with a single retry on failure. We come back to constrained decoding in Volume 2.

Natural Language Inference and the foundation for evaluation

Natural Language Inference (NLI) is the task of deciding, given two sentences, whether the first entails, contradicts, or is neutral with respect to the second. “A dog is running in the park” entails “An animal is outdoors.” It contradicts “There are no dogs anywhere.” It is neutral with respect to “It is raining.”

NLI is a venerable academic task with three classical datasets (SNLI, MultiNLI, ANLI). The reason it shows up in this chapter is that NLI models are the foundation of modern factuality evaluation.

The recipe: given an LLM-generated answer and a source passage, break the answer into atomic claims, and use an NLI model to check whether each claim is entailed by the source. If yes, the answer is faithful. If a claim is not entailed (or, worse, contradicted), the answer hallucinated.

from transformers import pipeline

nli = pipeline(
    "text-classification",
    model="MoritzLaurer/DeBERTa-v3-large-mnli-fever-anli-ling-wanli",
    top_k=None,
)

def is_entailed(premise, hypothesis):
    result = nli({"text": premise, "text_pair": hypothesis})[0]
    entail = next((s for s in result if s["label"] == "entailment"), None)
    return entail and entail["score"] > 0.5

This is what RAGAS’s faithfulness metric, every hallucination detector, and most factuality benchmarks use under the hood. The boring default NLI model is DeBERTa-v3-large-mnli-fever-anli or its 2026 successors.

Coreference, entity linking, relation extraction

The classical IE pipeline: figure out who’s who, link mentions to a knowledge base, extract structured relations. In the LLM era, all three are increasingly handled by an LLM with a prompt. They still matter as concepts because: (a) some systems still use specialized pipelines for cost/latency, and (b) the concepts show up in prompt design and evaluation.

Coreference resolution. “Tim Cook visited Microsoft. He praised their products.” Coreference says He = Tim Cook and their = Microsoft. The classical models are span-based neural networks (NeuralCoref, AllenNLP’s coref, then Maverick). Modern systems hand the task to an LLM or use it as a feature in entity-linking pipelines. spaCy’s coreferee is the boring default for off-the-shelf use.

Entity linking. Given a mention (“Tim Cook”), pick the right Wikipedia/Wikidata entity (Q295810, the Apple CEO, not some other Tim Cook). The classical pipeline is mention detection → candidate generation → ranking, with each step using either a learned model or a knowledge-base lookup. Modern systems use embedding-based retrieval against entity descriptions, plus an LLM reranker. Libraries: REL, BLINK, GENRE.

Relation extraction and knowledge-graph construction. Given text, extract (subject, relation, object) triples: ("Tim Cook", "is_CEO_of", "Apple"). Used to build knowledge graphs for downstream reasoning. The classical recipe (NER + dependency parse + rule-based template) is fast and brittle; modern recipes use an LLM with a structured-output prompt and dramatically outperform classical pipelines on complex relations.

In 2026, the boring default for “extract entities and relations from text” is one LLM call with a JSON schema. You only build a specialized pipeline if cost or latency demands it.

LLM evaluation frameworks

Evaluating generative outputs is the hardest production problem in this chapter. Exact-match doesn’t work “June 29th, 2007” and “June 29, 2007” are the same answer. F1 partially works but misses semantic equivalence. Human review doesn’t scale. The 2024-2026 answer is LLM-as-judge: use a strong LLM to score outputs against a rubric. It’s imperfect, biased, and the only production option that doesn’t require human labelers.

The big three: RAGAS, DeepEval, G-Eval

RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) is the boring default for evaluating RAG systems. It defines four metrics:

  • Faithfulness does each claim in the answer come from the retrieved context? (NLI-based)
  • Answer relevance does the answer address the question? (Reverse-question generation + similarity)
  • Context precision of retrieved chunks, what fraction were relevant?
  • Context recall did retrieval return everything needed for the gold answer?
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall

results = evaluate(
    dataset=eval_dataset,
    metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_precision, context_recall],
)

DeepEval is “pytest for LLMs”: every metric is a test case, integrates with CI/CD, supports custom metrics. Ships G-Eval (chain-of-thought-driven custom-criterion evaluation), task completion, hallucination, bias, and toxicity.

G-Eval (Liu et al. 2023) is both a method and a metric. The method: prompt an LLM with a custom evaluation criterion, ask it to first write out the steps it would use to evaluate, then apply those steps and produce a score. Empirically more reliable than direct scoring.

Why LLM-as-judge fails silently

Three failure modes worth knowing about.

Judge bias. LLMs preferentially score longer answers, answers from their own model family (“GPT-4 likes GPT-4”), and answers that match the rubric prose stylistically. If you can afford it, use a different model family for judging than for generating.

JSON parsing failures. When the judge’s output doesn’t parse, libraries silently exclude that case from the aggregate, which biases the score. Always log parse failures explicitly and treat them as a separate failure mode.

Drift over model versions. Upgrading the judge model changes every metric, which makes A/B tests across model versions meaningless. Freeze the judge model and version for the duration of your evaluation campaign; only upgrade deliberately, with re-baselining.

Calibration: the trust layer

Never trust the raw judge score until you’ve correlated it against human labels. The recipe:

  1. Sample 100 evaluation cases.
  2. Have humans label them on the same rubric the judge uses.
  3. Compute Spearman correlation between human and judge scores.
  4. If correlation is below 0.7, the rubric needs work or the judge isn’t capable enough.

This calibration step is the difference between “we have an evaluation pipeline” and “we have an evaluation pipeline we trust.” Skip it and you’ll deploy regressions that the judge missed.

We come back to LLM evaluation in detail in Volume 2 and Volume 3.

Long-context evaluation: needle in a haystack and beyond

Modern LLMs advertise context windows of 128k, 256k, even 1M+ tokens. Most of those numbers are aspirational the model technically processes the input, but accuracy degrades the further into the context the relevant information sits.

Needle in a haystack (NIAH) is the canonical long-context test. Insert a unique fact (“the magic password is electric-marsupial-7”) at a specific depth in a long document. Ask the model to recall it. Plot accuracy as a function of (context length × insertion depth).

NIAH is too easy for modern models. Most 2026 LLMs solve it almost perfectly up to their advertised window. The harder benchmarks:

  • RULER (NVIDIA): NIAH plus multi-needle, multi-hop tracing, aggregation tasks. Models that ace plain NIAH often fall apart on RULER.
  • LongBench and InfiniteBench: real-world long-context tasks (long-document QA, summarization, code).
  • NoLiMa: tests whether models can handle distracting irrelevant context (the “hay” mattering, not just the needle).

The 2026 finding: most LLMs “support” their full context length but only use effectively the first ~25-50%. Designing for the actual usable window not the marketing one is part of system design. Volume 2 covers long-context architectures and inference; here, the lesson is: always evaluate end-to-end on context lengths you actually use, with tasks that match your application.

Dialogue state tracking

In a multi-turn conversation, the system needs to track what’s been said: the user mentioned “next Monday” three turns ago, asked about restaurants two turns ago, and now wants to confirm the booking. Dialogue state tracking (DST) is the explicit representation of this.

Classical DST formulated state as a slot-filling problem: a fixed schema ({cuisine: ?, location: ?, time: ?, party_size: ?}) and a model that updated slots as the dialogue progressed. The standard datasets (MultiWOZ, SGD) fueled a decade of research.

Modern DST is largely absorbed into the LLM. The conversation history is the state; the LLM reads it and produces appropriate next actions. For complex assistants (especially agents that take real-world actions), explicit state still matters because LLMs forget, hallucinate, or contradict themselves over long contexts. The hybrid pattern, common in 2026:

  1. The LLM processes each user turn.
  2. A separate “state extractor” call updates an explicit state object (slots, intents, completed actions).
  3. The next LLM turn sees both the conversation and the structured state.

This is the memory layer of dialogue agents. Volume 2 covers it under “agent memory”; the conceptual ancestor is classical DST.

What you’d actually use

A summary of the boring defaults, by job:

Job Default Why
Subword tokenization Match the model’s tokenizer Always
Sentence embeddings (self-hosted) BGE-large or Nomic Embed v2 Strong, MIT
Sentence embeddings (API) OpenAI text-embedding-3 or Voyage 3 Quality, low latency
Document chunking RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, ~512 tokens Boring default that wins
Hybrid search BM25 + dense via RRF Production-grade
Reranker BGE Reranker v2-m3 or Cohere Rerank 3 Cross-encoder quality
Vector index Qdrant, Weaviate, or pgvector Open + production-ready
JSON output API json_schema mode + Pydantic validation Cheapest reliability
Constrained decoding (open-source) Outlines or guidance When grammar matters
NLI / entailment DeBERTa-v3-mnli-fever-anli The boring default
RAG evaluation RAGAS (4 metrics) + LLM-as-judge Industry standard
LLM-test infra DeepEval Pytest-style integration
Long-context eval RULER + your task-specific test set Marketing window != usable window
Dialogue memory Conversation log + structured state Hybrid pattern
NER / entity linking (off-the-shelf) spaCy or LLM with JSON schema LLM wins on accuracy
Coreference (when explicit) spaCy coreferee Adequate for most needs

If you forget everything else: chunk recursively at ~512 tokens, embed with BGE or OpenAI, retrieve hybrid (BM25 + dense), rerank with a cross-encoder, validate output with Pydantic, evaluate with RAGAS. That stack ships most of the production RAG in 2026.

Sharp edges

A short list of failure modes this chapter exists to prevent.

Tokenizer mismatch. Embedding documents with one tokenizer and querying with another. Always use the same tokenizer end to end.

Embedding model drift. Re-embedding production data with a new model without re-indexing means new and old embeddings live in the same space. The mismatch is silent and corrupts retrieval.

Chunking that loses headings. A chunk that says “the rate is 7%” without “Section 4: Tax Rates” above it has lost its meaning. Either include parents (parent-document chunking) or prepend section titles to chunks (contextual retrieval).

Re-ranking too small a candidate set. If your top-K from retrieval is 5 and the right answer is rank 12, the reranker can’t fix it. Set retrieval K to at least 20-50, then rerank to 3-5.

RAG evaluation without ground truth. RAGAS metrics that don’t require references (faithfulness, answer relevance) are noisy without a known-correct answer. Always have a hand-curated gold set, however small.

Trusting NIAH as long-context evidence. Marketing benchmarks are designed to be passed. Use RULER, NoLiMa, or your own task-specific tests.

Evaluating with the same model that generates. Self-evaluation is biased. Use a different model family for judging.

Pipeline complexity creep. Every additional stage (rewriter → retriever → reranker → cross-encoder → LLM) adds latency, failure modes, and cost. Start simple; add stages only when measurement justifies them.

Overlap as a default without testing. The 20% chunk-overlap default has no empirical support in many benchmarks. Try zero overlap and compare.

Forgetting the LLM-as-judge calibration loop. Without 50-100 hand-labeled examples and a Spearman correlation, you don’t know whether your evaluation is measuring quality or judge bias.

Where this goes next

Chapter 12 is the final chapter of Volume 1: speech and audio. The classical-era field that paralleled NLP through the deep-learning revolution and ended up with its own version of the same story handcrafted features, then HMMs, then end-to-end neural models, then transformers, then foundation models. By the end of Chapter 12 you will have completed the foundation: math, ML, deep learning, vision, NLP, audio.

Volume 2 then opens at the transformer and rewires everything you’ve learned through the lens of sequence modeling at scale. The vocabulary you built in Chapter 10 (tokenizers, embeddings, attention, encoder-decoder) and Chapter 11 (modern embeddings, RAG primitives, structured outputs, evaluation) becomes the vocabulary of the LLM era. Almost nothing here was wasted.

Open Chapter 12 when you’re ready.

Chapter 12 Speech and Audio

A startup in 2026 ships a customer-support voice agent. A user calls. Within 200 milliseconds of the user speaking the first word, the system has detected speech, transcribed the audio, identified the speaker, parsed the intent, generated a response, synthesized speech, and started streaming the reply back. The whole loop runs continuously: the agent can be interrupted, knows when the user has stopped speaking, handles the user talking over it, and switches between English and Spanish mid-sentence. By the time the call is over, the agent has handled the request, escalated the edge case, and logged a full transcript with sentiment markers and any compliance-relevant flags.

That pipeline didn’t exist three years earlier. The pieces existed speech recognition, speech synthesis, speaker verification, voice activity detection, turn-taking but they were independent academic subfields, each with its own architectures, datasets, and evaluation metrics. The 2024-2026 wave of foundation models for audio (Whisper for recognition, EnCodec/SoundStream for compression, audio LLMs for understanding, and end-to-end speech-to-speech models like Moshi and Hibiki) collapsed these into one stack. Most of what’s in this chapter, like most of what’s in Chapter 9 for vision, is a story about how audio became less plural.

This chapter is the foundation. Sound waves, sampling, spectrograms, the journey from feature-engineered ASR to Whisper, the parallel journey from concatenative TTS to neural voices, the production pipelines that wire everything together, and the 2026 frontier of streaming speech-to-speech and audio language models. Seventeen source lessons, one chapter, one voice. By the end of it, you’ll be able to build the voice agent in the opening paragraph yourself.

Why this chapter exists

Audio is the last sensory domain Volume 1 covers. Like vision and language, it was a hand-crafted-features field for forty years, became deep-learning-dominated around 2015, and converged on transformer-based foundation models around 2022. Like vision and language, the production pipeline in 2026 is a handful of pretrained checkpoints wired together with thin glue code.

If you skipped audio entirely, you’d miss four things that matter beyond audio itself:

  1. The mel spectrogram is the bridge between continuous waveforms and the kind of input neural networks like a 2-D image, basically. The same Fourier-transform machinery from Chapter 5 turns out to be the foundation of every audio model.
  2. CTC loss (Connectionist Temporal Classification) is the trick that made end-to-end ASR work, and it shows up again in handwriting recognition, lip reading, and any “input sequence to output sequence with unknown alignment” problem.
  3. Neural audio codecs discrete tokens for audio are what make audio LLMs possible. The same concept (continuous signal → discrete tokens → autoregressive model) is now being applied to images, video, and any other sensor modality.
  4. The streaming/real-time constraint is the audio engineer’s permanent reality, and it teaches lessons about latency, buffering, and incremental inference that translate directly to LLM serving in Volume 3.

By the end of this chapter, you’ll have crossed every modality Volume 1 promised, and Volume 2’s transformer will be the unifying architecture that ties it all together.

Audio fundamentals: what sound is to a computer

Sound is a pressure wave traveling through air. A microphone converts pressure variations into voltage; an analog-to-digital converter samples that voltage at fixed intervals and stores the samples as numbers. The result is a one-dimensional array of samples a waveform.

Three numbers define the digital representation:

Sample rate how many samples per second. CD-quality audio is 44.1 kHz (44,100 samples/second). Modern speech systems usually use 16 kHz; music and high-fidelity audio use 48 kHz. By the Nyquist-Shannon theorem, you can represent any frequency up to half the sample rate; 16 kHz audio can capture frequencies up to 8 kHz, which covers all of human speech but cuts off the very top of orchestra music.

Bit depth how many bits per sample. 16-bit is standard. Each sample is an integer in [−32768,32767][-32768, 32767][−32768,32767] (or a float in [−1,1][-1, 1][−1,1] after normalization). 24-bit gives more dynamic range; 32-bit float is what most ML pipelines use internally.

Channels mono (one channel), stereo (two), 5.1 surround (six). Speech is almost always processed as mono; music and immersive audio use stereo or more.

A 10-second mono speech clip at 16 kHz is a 160,000-sample float32 array 640 KB. A 10-second stereo song at 44.1 kHz 16-bit is ~1.7 MB. These are big enough to be worth caring about; they’re small enough that processing one clip on CPU is instantaneous. The tensor shape that PyTorch expects is (batch, channels, samples).

import torchaudio
waveform, sample_rate = torchaudio.load("speech.wav")
print(waveform.shape, sample_rate)   # torch.Size([1, 160000]) 16000

The two formats you’ll meet most: WAV (uncompressed, the boring default for ML pipelines) and MP3 / AAC / Opus (lossy compressed, common in production media). Always decode to raw waveform tensors before feeding to a model; compression artifacts compose with model errors in unpredictable ways.

Resampling, normalization, mono conversion

Pretrained audio models expect specific input rates and formats. Whisper expects 16 kHz mono float32 in [−1,1][-1, 1][−1,1]. Music models typically expect 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz stereo. Always resample to whatever your model expects.

import torchaudio.transforms as T

if sample_rate != 16_000:
    waveform = T.Resample(sample_rate, 16_000)(waveform)
if waveform.shape[0] > 1:
    waveform = waveform.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)   # stereo -> mono
waveform = waveform / waveform.abs().max().clamp(min=1e-6)  # peak-normalize to [-1, 1]

Three lines, but every audio pipeline needs them, and forgetting any of them is a silent quality loss.

Spectrograms and mel features

A waveform is a function of time. To extract structure useful for a neural network, you transform to the frequency domain. From Chapter 5: the Fourier transform decomposes a signal into the sum of sinusoids at different frequencies. For audio, you take the FFT of short overlapping windows of the waveform. The result is a spectrogram a 2-D array where one axis is time, the other is frequency, and each value is the magnitude of that frequency at that time.

The standard recipe is the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT):

  1. Slide a window (e.g., 25 ms) across the waveform with a hop (e.g., 10 ms).
  2. Multiply each window by a tapered function (Hann, Hamming) to reduce edge artifacts.
  3. Compute the FFT of each window.
  4. Take the magnitude (or power) of each FFT bin.
import torchaudio.transforms as T
spectrogram = T.Spectrogram(n_fft=400, hop_length=160, power=2.0)(waveform)
# Shape: (channels, freq_bins, time_frames)

For 16 kHz audio with n_fft=400, hop_length=160: each frame is 25 ms, hops are 10 ms, and you get 201 frequency bins.

The mel scale

Human hearing isn’t linear in frequency. We perceive a doubling of frequency (an octave) as a constant change, not a fixed Hz step. The mel scale is a perceptual transformation that maps Hz to mels in a way that matches how we hear: linear below 1 kHz, logarithmic above.

A mel spectrogram is a regular spectrogram with its frequency bins re-binned onto the mel scale, typically into 80 or 128 bins. It’s what every modern speech model takes as input.

mel = T.MelSpectrogram(
    sample_rate=16_000, n_fft=400, hop_length=160, n_mels=80,
)(waveform)
log_mel = torch.log(mel + 1e-6)   # log compression makes the dynamic range manageable

The output is an 80×T matrix per audio clip, where T is the number of time frames. This is what Whisper, Wav2Vec, HuBERT, and every other speech foundation model takes as input. The waveform is rarely fed directly anymore; the mel spectrogram is the de facto audio “image.”

A common further transformation is to extract MFCCs (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) apply a discrete cosine transform to the log-mel spectrogram and keep the first 13-40 coefficients. MFCCs were the boring default for classical speech recognition and speaker verification through ~2015; they’re rare in modern systems, which take the log-mel directly.

Why this works

Two intuitions worth internalizing.

A spectrogram looks like a picture, and CNNs/transformers can process it like one. The y-axis encodes frequency, the x-axis encodes time, and patterns in the 2-D image (formant traces in vowels, broadband noise in fricatives, harmonic structure in voiced speech) are visually distinct. A model that learned to classify images can be fine-tuned to classify spectrograms with minimal architectural change.

Most of speech information lives below 4 kHz. Telephone-quality speech (8 kHz sampling, 4 kHz max frequency) is intelligible, and most ASR systems work well on it. Music and environmental sounds need the full bandwidth.

Audio classification

Once audio is a 2-D image, classification is a Chapter-8 problem. Pick a backbone (ResNet, ViT, or a domain-specific model like AST Audio Spectrogram Transformer), feed log-mel spectrograms, train a head. The standard datasets:

  • AudioSet 2M clips with 527 sound classes (music, speech, environmental).
  • ESC-50 2,000 environmental sound clips, 50 classes.
  • UrbanSound8K 8,000 urban sound clips, 10 classes.

For most production audio classification (sound events, scene recognition, alert detection), the boring default is PANNs (Pretrained Audio Neural Networks) or AST (Audio Spectrogram Transformer), fine-tuned on your task. YAMNet is Google’s mobile-friendly default for on-device sound classification.

import torch
from transformers import ASTForAudioClassification, AutoFeatureExtractor

feat = AutoFeatureExtractor.from_pretrained("MIT/ast-finetuned-audioset-10-10-0.4593")
model = ASTForAudioClassification.from_pretrained("MIT/ast-finetuned-audioset-10-10-0.4593")
inputs = feat(waveform.squeeze().numpy(), sampling_rate=16_000, return_tensors="pt")
with torch.no_grad():
    logits = model(**inputs).logits
print(model.config.id2label[logits.argmax(-1).item()])

Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)

Speech recognition is the canonical audio task: turn a speech waveform into a text transcript. The journey from “GMM-HMM with hand-crafted features” to “Whisper, a transformer trained on 680,000 hours of multilingual speech” is the audio version of the deep-learning story.

The classical pipeline

For thirty years, ASR was a pipeline of three modules.

  1. Acoustic model given a slice of audio (typically 25 ms), predict a phoneme or sub-phoneme label. Classical: Gaussian Mixture Model + Hidden Markov Model (GMM-HMM). Mid-2010s: deep neural networks replaced GMMs (DNN-HMM).
  2. Pronunciation model a dictionary mapping words to phoneme sequences (“cat” → /k/ /æ/ /t/).
  3. Language model assigns probabilities to word sequences. Classical: n-gram models. Modern: neural LMs.

These three modules were combined with a Viterbi decoder that searched the joint space of acoustic frames, phonemes, and word sequences for the most likely transcript. Building this stack was a craft; tuning it was a career.

Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC)

The first major shift was CTC loss (Graves et al. 2006). CTC is a way to train a neural network on (audio, transcript) pairs without requiring frame-level alignment. The model outputs a per-frame distribution over characters (or phonemes), including a special “blank” symbol; the loss marginalizes over all alignments that collapse to the target transcript.

In simple terms, here’s the problem CTC solves. You have audio that’s 250 frames long and a transcript that’s 5 characters. The model produces 250 per-frame predictions, but you don’t know which frame produced which character and frames don’t always correspond to characters one-to-one (a long vowel might span 30 frames; a fricative might span 5). CTC handles this by introducing a “blank” symbol the model can emit on any frame, and a collapse rule: consecutive duplicate characters merge into one, and blanks are deleted. So cc--aa-tt and c-a-t--- and --ccaatt- all collapse to cat. The CTC loss sums the probabilities of every frame-level sequence that collapses to the target, which lets the model find its own alignment during training.

This single trick let the model emit blanks and collapse repeats is why end-to-end ASR became practical. The same trick recurs in handwriting recognition, lip reading, and any task where you have an input sequence and a shorter output sequence with unknown alignment.

The ASR architecture that won the late-2010s DeepSpeech 2 (Baidu 2015), then wav2letter and many descendants was a CNN/RNN stack trained with CTC loss. Decoding combined the per-frame output with an external n-gram language model. Quality matched or exceeded the classical pipeline at a fraction of the engineering effort.

Encoder-decoder ASR and the attention era

The next shift was attention-based encoder-decoder ASR (LAS Listen, Attend, Spell, Chan et al. 2015). The encoder is an RNN/CNN over audio frames; the decoder is an autoregressive RNN that generates the transcript one character at a time, attending to the encoder states. This eliminated the external language model the decoder learned a language model implicitly.

Hybrid CTC + attention models combined both losses for stability and best-of-both quality. This was the state of the art into 2020.

Wav2Vec 2.0 and self-supervised pretraining

In 2020, Facebook’s wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al.) showed that self-supervised pretraining on unlabeled audio dramatically reduced the labeled data needed for ASR. The recipe: train a transformer to predict masked regions of audio (the audio analogue of BERT’s masked language modeling), then fine-tune on a small labeled dataset.

The same pattern recurred in HuBERT (Hsu et al. 2021), WavLM, Data2Vec, and others. By 2022, you could match decade-old commercial ASR quality with 100 hours of labeled speech instead of 10,000.

Whisper: the foundation model for speech

In September 2022, OpenAI released Whisper (Radford et al.). The architecture is a vanilla transformer encoder-decoder operating on log-mel spectrograms. The training data is 680,000 hours of weakly-labeled multilingual audio scraped from the web. The model is trained jointly on transcription, translation (any language → English), and language identification, with all three encoded as text prompts.

Whisper-large-v3 is ~1.5B parameters. It transcribes 100 languages, translates them to English, identifies the language automatically, and handles long audio (chunked into 30-second windows) with timestamps. It’s the first audio model that “just works” out of the box on a wide range of inputs accents, noise, music, multiple speakers without fine-tuning.

import whisper

model = whisper.load_model("large-v3")
result = model.transcribe("audio.wav")
print(result["text"])
print(result["segments"])   # word- and sentence-level timestamps

The 2026 boring default for ASR is Whisper-large-v3 when quality matters and Whisper-large-v3-turbo or Distil-Whisper when speed and cost matter (10-30x faster, 1-2 percentage points worse WER). For real-time streaming, Whisper Streaming and faster-whisper are the production wrappers.

For specialized domains (medical, legal, low-resource languages), you fine-tune Whisper on labeled data. The Hugging Face ecosystem ships scripts for this; a few hundred labeled hours typically suffice.

Modern alternatives

Whisper isn’t the only game. Conformer-based models (combining convolution with self-attention) are still the choice for low-latency streaming ASR Apple’s Siri uses a Conformer, Google’s RNN-T-based streaming ASR uses similar primitives. Canary (NVIDIA 2024) is competitive with Whisper-large at smaller sizes. SeamlessM4T (Meta) handles 100+ languages with translation and speech-to-speech.

For the average production deployment in 2026, the tree is:

Need Choice
English, batch transcription Whisper-large-v3
Multilingual, batch Whisper-large-v3 (100 languages)
Streaming, low latency faster-whisper, Conformer-based
Edge / mobile Whisper-tiny, Distil-Whisper, Wav2Vec-tiny
Custom domain Fine-tune Whisper on your data

Speaker recognition and verification

Two related tasks: speaker identification (which of a known set of speakers said this?) and speaker verification (is this the same speaker as that?). Both reduce to learning speaker embeddings vectors that capture a speaker’s voice characteristics independent of what they’re saying.

The classical recipe was MFCC features + a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM-UBM, the “universal background model”). The deep-learning recipe is x-vectors (Snyder et al. 2018): train a TDNN (time-delay neural network) on speaker classification, then take the penultimate layer as the embedding. Modern recipes use ECAPA-TDNN or transformer encoders trained with a contrastive loss similar to CLIP pull together pairs of utterances from the same speaker, push apart pairs from different speakers.

from speechbrain.pretrained import EncoderClassifier
classifier = EncoderClassifier.from_hparams(source="speechbrain/spkrec-ecapa-voxceleb")
embedding = classifier.encode_batch(waveform)   # (1, 1, 192)

The output is a 192- or 256-dim vector per audio clip. Two clips’ similarity is cosine similarity. A threshold on similarity gives verification; nearest-neighbor over a database of enrolled speakers gives identification.

Production uses include voice authentication (banking, smart home), speaker diarization in call recordings (“who said what when”), and meeting transcription. The 2026 boring default for self-hosted is SpeechBrain ECAPA-TDNN; for managed APIs, Google Cloud Speech, Azure Speaker Recognition, or Pyannote.

Speaker diarization combines speaker embeddings with clustering: for a multi-speaker recording, segment it into utterances, embed each utterance, cluster the embeddings, and label each utterance with its cluster. Pyannote.audio 3.0+ is the standard library and the boring default.

Text-to-Speech (TTS)

Going the other direction text in, speech out is Text-to-Speech. The journey rhymes with ASR’s.

Concatenative and parametric TTS

Pre-2015 TTS came in two flavors. Concatenative TTS recorded a single voice actor reading thousands of utterances, segmented them into phonetic units, and at synthesis time stitched units together to form the target sentence. Quality was high (it was real human audio), but the voice was rigid you couldn’t change it, change the emotion, or add prosody not in the recordings.

Parametric TTS predicted acoustic features (mel spectrograms, F0 contours) from text using a model, then synthesized audio with a vocoder. Quality was lower than concatenative but the system was more flexible. Hidden Markov Model TTS (HMM-TTS) was the classical version.

Neural TTS: Tacotron and beyond

In 2017, Google’s Tacotron 2 (Wang et al.) showed end-to-end neural TTS could match concatenative quality. The pipeline: text → encoder-decoder transformer → mel spectrogram → WaveNet vocoder → waveform. Two-stage neural pipelines became the standard.

FastSpeech (Microsoft 2019) replaced the autoregressive decoder with a non-autoregressive one predict the entire mel spectrogram in parallel. 100x faster inference, comparable quality. FastSpeech 2 added explicit prosody control (pitch, energy, duration). Both used a separate vocoder (WaveNet, WaveGlow, HiFi-GAN) to convert mel → waveform.

VITS (Kim et al. 2021) integrated the two stages: a single end-to-end model with a flow-based decoder that produced waveforms directly from text. Faster, smaller, comparable quality. The boring default for self-hosted neural TTS through 2023.

Foundation TTS: VALL-E, Bark, XTTS, OpenVoice, Sesame

The 2023-2026 wave brought TTS into the foundation-model era. VALL-E (Microsoft 2023) used a neural audio codec (EnCodec) to discretize audio, then trained a language model on (text, audio-token) pairs. With 3 seconds of reference audio from a target speaker, VALL-E could synthesize that speaker saying anything zero-shot voice cloning.

Bark (Suno AI 2023) was the first widely-used open-source equivalent. XTTS (Coqui 2023) extended to multi-language. OpenVoice (MyShell 2024) emphasized voice cloning quality. Sesame (2024) and CSM (Conversational Speech Model, 2025) integrated TTS with conversational context. Voicebox and NaturalSpeech 3 are Meta and Microsoft’s research entries.

By 2026 the production TTS landscape is:

Need Choice
API, premium quality ElevenLabs, OpenAI tts-1-hd
Self-hosted, multi-speaker XTTS-v2, OpenVoice
Self-hosted, single voice VITS or FastSpeech 2
Real-time streaming Sesame CSM, Cartesia Sonic
Edge / mobile Piper TTS

ElevenLabs deserves a callout: it’s the API quality leader for emotional, multilingual, fine-grained-control synthesis, and it’s what most production voice agents use when budget allows.

Voice cloning, voice conversion

Voice cloning synthesizes a target speaker’s voice from a short sample. Voice conversion changes the speaker identity of existing speech without changing the content. Both are active research areas with serious deployment in production (audiobooks, accessibility, dubbing) and serious abuse potential (deepfake voice scams). We come back to this under anti-spoofing.

Music generation

Generating music is harder than generating speech. Speech has a tight structure (phonemes, words) and a small set of learnable patterns; music has long-range dependencies, multiple simultaneous instruments, and a much wider expressive range. The 2024-2026 frontier:

MusicGen (Meta 2023) uses a single transformer over discrete audio codec tokens (EnCodec) to generate music conditioned on text and/or melody. AudioCraft (the library) ships MusicGen plus AudioGen (sound effects) and EnCodec.

Stable Audio (Stability AI 2024+) uses latent diffusion (the same architecture family as Stable Diffusion for images) to generate music up to 3 minutes long.

Suno and Udio are the 2026 generation leaders for full-song generation text-to-song, complete with vocals, lyrics, and arrangement. Both are closed APIs.

Riffusion is the curious genre-of-one that runs Stable Diffusion on spectrograms and converts back to audio. Quality is uneven but the trick is illustrative.

For most engineers in 2026, music generation is API territory. Self-hosted MusicGen and Stable Audio are good for non-vocal music; vocal song generation is closed-source.

Audio Language Models

The 2024+ wave that integrates audio into the LLM stack the same way vision did with VLMs. The pattern is identical: an audio encoder produces tokens, a projector maps them into LLM space, an LLM decodes text (or audio).

Three architectural families:

Audio in, text out. The audio analog of CLIP-grounded VLMs. Models: SALMONN, Qwen2-Audio, GAMA, Audio-Flamingo. Use cases: audio captioning, sound event description, audio QA.

Speech in, text out (with reasoning). Whisper extended with an LLM-style understanding head. Qwen2-Audio, Phi-4-Multimodal, the audio side of GPT-4o. Use cases: voice assistants that don’t lose nuance through ASR, multilingual speech understanding.

Speech in, speech out (full duplex). End-to-end models that take audio and emit audio. Moshi (Kyutai 2024), Hibiki (Kyutai 2025), the audio side of GPT-4o-realtime. Use cases: voice agents with sub-second latency, simultaneous translation.

The decisive shift here is neural audio codecs as discrete tokenizers. Instead of treating audio as continuous waveforms or spectrograms, you encode it as a sequence of discrete tokens (typically 25-50 tokens per second across 8-32 codebooks), and then any sequence model that handles text can also handle audio. The token sequence is the bridge.

Neural audio codecs

A traditional audio codec (MP3, AAC, Opus) compresses audio for efficient transmission. A neural audio codec does the same thing with a neural network: encode waveform into a sequence of discrete tokens, decode the tokens back to waveform. Quality at low bitrates is dramatically better than classical codecs.

SoundStream (Google 2021) was the first practical neural codec; EnCodec (Meta 2022) is the open-source production default. Both use a CNN encoder + residual vector quantization + CNN decoder, trained adversarially.

from encodec import EncodecModel
codec = EncodecModel.encodec_model_24khz()
codec.set_target_bandwidth(6.0)   # 6 kbps
encoded_frames = codec.encode(waveform)
codes = encoded_frames[0][0]   # (B, n_q, T)   discrete tokens, n_q quantizers

A 24 kHz mono waveform at 6 kbps becomes 75 frames per second × 8 codebooks × 10-bit codes. The tokens are what audio LLMs consume and produce.

Mimi (Kyutai) and DAC (Descript Audio Codec) are 2024-era successors with better quality at lower bitrates. The codec layer is now a foundational primitive in the audio stack, on the same level as the byte-pair encoder for text.

To make the connection concrete: the same architectural pattern that lets text LLMs work take continuous data, discretize to tokens, train an autoregressive sequence model on the tokens, decode tokens back to data is what neural codecs enable for audio. Once your speech is a sequence of discrete tokens, every transformer trick from Volume 2 (KV cache, FlashAttention, speculative decoding, chain-of-thought) applies. That generalization is why audio LLMs, image LLMs, video LLMs, and the whole “any modality is just tokens” research direction took off in 2024.

The catch is that audio codecs typically use multiple parallel codebooks (8-32 quantizers per frame), so the token rate is high 50 fps × 8 codebooks = 400 tokens per second of audio. This is one reason audio LLMs are computationally expensive even when their model sizes seem small: the sequences are long. We’ll come back to long-sequence handling in Volume 2.

Real-time audio processing

Most of the audio applications worth building in 2026 are real-time: voice assistants, live translation, transcription as you speak. Real-time imposes hard constraints that batch-mode pipelines never face.

Streaming, latency, buffering

A real-time audio pipeline looks like:

microphone -> (audio frames every 20-30 ms) -> VAD -> ASR (chunk-based) -> LLM (token-streamed) -> TTS (chunk-based) -> speaker

Each stage adds latency. The total budget for a “conversational” feel is ~300-500 ms from end-of-user-speech to start-of-response. Each stage has to be designed to:

  • Process incrementally start producing output as soon as it has enough input, not after the user is done.
  • Stream output deliver partial results so downstream stages can start working.
  • Recover from chunk boundaries overlap, lookahead, or context-carry to avoid mistakes at frame boundaries.

Whisper, by default, is not real-time it processes 30-second chunks. faster-whisper with condition_on_previous_text and chunk overlap gets close. Whisper-streaming and Riva (NVIDIA’s commercial speech stack) handle true streaming.

Voice Activity Detection (VAD) and turn-taking

A voice agent needs to know when the user started speaking, when they stopped, and whether the silence is “end of turn” or “thinking pause.” This is Voice Activity Detection (VAD) plus endpointing plus turn-taking.

Silero VAD is the boring default a small (~1 MB) pretrained model that returns a per-chunk probability of speech. WebRTC VAD is the classical alternative, lighter and lower quality. NVIDIA Marble is a 2024-era model focused on end-of-turn detection a harder problem than plain VAD because pauses can mean “still my turn” or “your turn now.”

import torch
model, utils = torch.hub.load(repo_or_dir='snakers4/silero-vad', model='silero_vad')
get_speech_timestamps = utils[0]

speech_segments = get_speech_timestamps(waveform, model, sampling_rate=16_000)

Turn-taking is the harder cousin of VAD. The 2024-2026 frontier ships models that handle barge-in (the user interrupting the agent), backchannels (“uh-huh”), and overlapping speech. Moshi and Hibiki integrate turn-taking into the speech-to-speech model itself.

The voice assistant pipeline, end to end

The full production voice-agent loop:

1. Audio frames every 20 ms from microphone
2. VAD per frame; buffer accumulates active speech
3. Streaming ASR transcribes incrementally with timestamps
4. End-of-turn detector decides when the user has finished
5. LLM consumes the transcript (with conversation history, tool definitions) and generates a response
6. Token stream from LLM is fed to streaming TTS
7. TTS generates audio chunks; chunks are streamed to speaker
8. If user starts speaking again (VAD), interrupt the TTS stream

Eight stages, each of which can be a separate service. The 2026 production stack typically uses:

  • Audio capture: WebRTC, Twilio, or platform SDK
  • VAD: Silero or Marble
  • ASR: faster-whisper or Whisper-streaming
  • LLM: GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, or open-source via vLLM
  • TTS: ElevenLabs, OpenAI tts-1, or Cartesia Sonic
  • Orchestration: LiveKit Agents, Pipecat, Vocode

Or you skip the pipeline and use an integrated speech-to-speech model. Moshi (Kyutai 2024) and Hibiki (Kyutai 2025) are open-source full-duplex models they take audio in and produce audio out, no intermediate text. Latency is ~150-300 ms; quality is below stage-pipeline systems but improving fast. GPT-4o realtime is the closed-API equivalent. By the late 2020s, end-to-end models will likely replace the pipeline; in 2026 the pipeline is still the boring default for most production voice agents.

A skeletal voice-agent loop, with all stages glued together, looks like this:

import asyncio
import numpy as np
import torch
from faster_whisper import WhisperModel
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

# 1. Load models once at startup
asr  = WhisperModel("large-v3", device="cuda", compute_type="float16")
vad, vad_utils = torch.hub.load("snakers4/silero-vad", "silero_vad")
llm = AsyncOpenAI()

async def voice_agent(audio_in_queue, audio_out_queue, history):
    buffer = np.zeros(0, dtype=np.float32)
    while True:
        chunk = await audio_in_queue.get()         # 30 ms of audio
        buffer = np.concatenate([buffer, chunk])
        if not is_speech(buffer[-30 * 16:], vad):  # check last 30 ms
            if len(buffer) > 16_000:               # at least 1 sec collected
                segments, _ = asr.transcribe(buffer, language="en", beam_size=1)
                user_text = " ".join(s.text for s in segments)
                buffer = np.zeros(0, dtype=np.float32)

                history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_text})
                stream = await llm.chat.completions.create(
                    model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=history, stream=True,
                )
                response_text = ""
                async for delta in stream:
                    token = delta.choices[0].delta.content or ""
                    response_text += token
                    if any(p in token for p in ".?!"):
                        audio = await synthesize_streaming(response_text)
                        await audio_out_queue.put(audio)
                        response_text = ""
                history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response_text})

Eight stages collapsed into 30 lines. Real production code adds error handling, interruption logic, tool calls, state management, and observability. The shape is the same.

Anti-spoofing and watermarking

Voice cloning at human-quality is a real capability with real abuse vectors: voice phishing, deepfake fraud, impersonation. Two technical countermeasures.

Anti-spoofing detects synthetic speech. The 2025-2026 generation of detectors (e.g., AASIST, RawNet3) train on real-vs-synthetic speech corpora and reach 95%+ accuracy on clean audio. Quality drops on out-of-distribution voice cloning systems, which is why the field is in a permanent arms race.

Audio watermarking embeds a robust signal in synthesized audio that survives compression, replay, and re-recording. AudioSeal (Meta 2024) is the open-source default; ElevenLabs, OpenAI, and other commercial TTS providers ship their own watermarks. The signal is invisible to humans, robust to common transformations, and detectable with a verifier. Watermarking is required by an increasing list of regulators (EU AI Act, California’s AB-1008) for any synthetic media.

In production, the responsible TTS stack ships with watermarking enabled by default, plus a separate anti-spoofing detector for incoming audio (to catch impersonation attempts on inbound voice authentication).

Audio evaluation metrics

Audio shares some of NLP’s evaluation pain (generative outputs are hard to score) and adds its own.

ASR evaluation uses Word Error Rate (WER) and Character Error Rate (CER). WER is the edit distance between predicted and reference transcripts, normalized by reference length. Lower is better; 5% WER is near-human for clean English speech, 20% is rough. The standard is to compute WER on a benchmark like LibriSpeech (clean read speech), CommonVoice (multilingual), or in-domain test sets.

TTS evaluation is harder. Objective metrics include Mel Cepstral Distortion (MCD) and learned metrics like UTMOS (a model that predicts human MOS scores). The gold standard is MOS (Mean Opinion Score) humans rate samples 1-5 on naturalness and intelligibility. Like all subjective metrics, MOS is noisy and expensive; CrowdMOS and pairwise comparisons (which sample sounds better, A or B?) are common in practice.

Speaker verification uses Equal Error Rate (EER) the error rate at the threshold where false-accept rate equals false-reject rate.

Music generation is hardest. FAD (Fréchet Audio Distance, the audio cousin of FID for images), CLAP-Score (text-audio similarity), and human ratings.

Audio LLMs evaluate on a mix: WER on transcription tasks, BLEU on translation, MOS on speech generation, and task-specific accuracy on benchmarks like AudioBench, Dynamic-SUPERB, and SLU benchmarks.

The general rule, same as Chapter 9: combine objective metrics with human evaluation, and don’t trust any single number.

What you’d actually use

A summary of the 2026 boring defaults, by job:

Job Default Why
Audio loading torchaudio Standard, fast
Resampling, mel features torchaudio.transforms One-line transforms
Audio classification AST or PANNs Pretrained, accurate
ASR (batch, English) Whisper-large-v3 Foundation-model quality
ASR (batch, multilingual) Whisper-large-v3 (100 languages) Same model
ASR (real-time) faster-whisper or Whisper-streaming Production-ready
ASR (edge) Distil-Whisper or Wav2Vec2-base Small, fast
Speaker recognition SpeechBrain ECAPA-TDNN Open, strong
Diarization Pyannote.audio 3.x Standard library
TTS (premium) ElevenLabs API Quality leader
TTS (self-hosted, multi-speaker) XTTS-v2 or OpenVoice Open
TTS (real-time) Sesame CSM or Cartesia Sonic Sub-second
Voice cloning OpenVoice or XTTS-v2 Open, capable
Music generation Suno / Udio API; MusicGen self-hosted API quality, MusicGen open
Audio codec EnCodec Standard for audio LLM tokens
VAD Silero Tiny, accurate
End-of-turn detection NVIDIA Marble Beyond plain VAD
Voice agent orchestration LiveKit Agents, Pipecat, Vocode Production stacks
Speech-to-speech Moshi / Hibiki (open) or GPT-4o realtime (API) End-to-end pipeline replacement
Audio watermarking AudioSeal Meta open-source
Anti-spoofing AASIST or RawNet3 Standard detectors
Audio LLM Qwen2-Audio, SALMONN, Phi-4-Multimodal Audio understanding
Evaluation WER, MOS, FAD, CLAP-Score Plus human eval

If you forget everything else: mel spectrogram + Whisper for input, audio LLM or text LLM for reasoning, ElevenLabs or XTTS for output, Silero for VAD, EnCodec for any token-based work, LiveKit/Pipecat for the orchestration. That stack ships most of the production voice work in 2026.

Sharp edges

A list of the failures this chapter exists to prevent.

Sample-rate mismatch. Loading 44.1 kHz audio and feeding it to a 16-kHz-trained ASR model. Output is gibberish. Always resample to model expectations.

Mel parameters. A model trained with n_mels=80, n_fft=400, hop_length=160 will not work with n_mels=128. Match the feature extraction exactly.

Whisper’s 30-second window. Whisper processes audio in 30-second chunks. Long audio without proper chunking either hallucinates or truncates. Use the official transcription pipeline or faster-whisper with VAD-based chunking.

Real-time vs batch confusion. A model that gets 5% WER offline can get 15% WER streaming, because it doesn’t have full-utterance context. Always evaluate in the deployment mode you’ll use.

Speaker embedding cosine vs euclidean. Speaker embeddings are normalized; use cosine similarity, not euclidean.

Diarization on overlapping speech. Most diarization systems assume one speaker at a time. Overlap (two people talking) breaks them. Use overlap-aware diarization (Pyannote 3+) when this matters.

TTS hallucinations. Modern neural TTS sometimes drops words, repeats words, or adds sounds not in the input. Always validate critical output (numbers, names, URLs) by re-transcribing TTS output and comparing to input.

Voice clone consent. Voice cloning a person without consent is illegal in many jurisdictions and unethical everywhere. The libraries don’t enforce this; you must.

Latency under load. A voice agent with 200 ms latency in dev becomes 600 ms latency under production load. Always benchmark with realistic concurrency and network conditions.

Codec lossiness. EnCodec at 6 kbps is good; EnCodec at 1.5 kbps loses speaker identity. Match bitrate to use case.

Music copyright. Generated music and voice clones land in real copyright territory. Audit your training data and your output policies.

Where this goes next

This chapter closes Volume 1. Twelve chapters from “what is AI engineering?” through every modality the field touches: math, machine learning, deep learning, vision, NLP, audio. By now, when someone in Volume 2 says “feed the audio embedding through the projector into the LLM,” nothing in that sentence will be unfamiliar.

Volume 2 opens at the transformer the architecture that quietly took over every modality you’ve just toured. By the end of Volume 2’s first chapter, you’ll see why the same model that classifies images can transcribe audio, generate text, and answer questions about all three at once. The pieces have been local; the unification is about to begin.

Open Volume 2 when you’re ready. You’ve earned it.
